proposals.title: A Pragmatic, Multi-Center Cohort Study Comparing Imipenem-Cilastatin-Relebactam Monotherapy versus Combination Regimens for Infections Caused by Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterobacterales
proposals.motivation: Infections caused by carbapenem-resistant gram-negative bacteria (CR-GNB), particularly CRPA and CRE, are associated with high mortality, especially in vulnerable populations like oncology and nursing home patients. The clinical dogma that combination therapy is always superior for highly resistant pathogens drives broad-spectrum antibiotic use, increasing toxicity, cost, and resistance pressure, yet robust comparative effectiveness data for newer agents like IMI/REL are lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: For serious infections caused by IMI/REL-susceptible CRPA and CRE, clinical outcomes (all-cause 30-day mortality) with IMI/REL monotherapy are non-inferior to outcomes achieved with IMI/REL-based combination regimens.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using data from 2018-2025. The primary exposure is IMI/REL monotherapy vs. IMI/REL + a second active agent (e.g., aminoglycoside, polymyxin). The primary outcome is all-cause 30-day mortality. We will use propensity score matching (PSM) to balance groups on key confounders (e.g., source of infection, severity scores, prior antibiotic exposure, underlying malignancy). A multivariable logistic regression model, adjusted for residual confounding after PSM, will test for non-inferiority with a pre-specified margin of 10%.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide urgently needed real-world evidence to guide antimicrobial stewardship for CR-GNB, potentially supporting effective de-escalation to monotherapy. It will directly inform treatment guidelines for high-risk patient groups and help reduce unnecessary antibiotic exposure, aligning with Dr. Kaye's research on resistant infections and patient outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared combinations to older monotherapies, this work directly challenges the combination dogma for a modern, potent β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor agent (IMI/REL) in a high-stakes, real-world setting, focusing on a contemporary cohort of infections defined by precise resistance mechanisms.

proposals.title: Long-term Surveillance for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Colonization and Mortality Following Imipenem-Cilastatin-Relebactam Treatment for Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam (IMI/REL) is a critical new agent for treating carbapenem-resistant gram-negative infections like HABP/VABP. However, its long-term ecological impact on patient gut colonization with resistant pathogens and subsequent mortality risk remains poorly characterized, especially in high-risk populations such as oncology and transplant patients. Systematic post-treatment surveillance is essential to inform antimicrobial stewardship and prevent unintended consequences of novel antibiotic use.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that patients treated with IMI/REL for HABP/VABP will have a significant incidence of subsequent gastrointestinal colonization with carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Enterobacterales (CRE), and that this colonization will be an independent risk factor for long-term all-cause mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective cohort study of adult patients completing a course of IMI/REL for HABP/VABP. Serial rectal swabs will be collected at treatment completion, and at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months post-treatment. Swabs will be cultured for CRPA and CRE, with isolates undergoing whole-genome sequencing. Patient survival will be tracked for 12 months. We will use competing-risk regression models, adjusting for prior antibiotic exposure, comorbidities, and neutropenia, to assess the association between resistant colonization and mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal data on the gut reservoir of multi-drug resistant organisms following IMI/REL therapy, directly addressing a key safety concern. It will quantify the long-term mortality risk associated with post-treatment colonization, offering evidence to refine stewardship guidelines and post-discharge monitoring protocols for vulnerable patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye et al. has focused on the epidemiology of resistance in institutional settings or during treatment, this proposal uniquely applies long-term, patient-level molecular surveillance specifically after a novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor therapy, linking microbiological outcomes to long-term survival.

proposals.title: A Machine Learning Model for Early Prediction of 28-Day All-Cause Mortality in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Bloodstream Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) infections, particularly bloodstream infections, are associated with high mortality rates, especially in vulnerable populations like nursing home residents and oncology patients. Current risk stratification tools are often static and lack the ability to integrate complex, time-varying clinical and microbiological data from the first 72 hours post-diagnosis. An accurate, early predictive model could enable more aggressive, personalized intervention strategies for the highest-risk patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model trained on clinical, laboratory, and microbiological variables collected within the first 72 hours of a CRPA bloodstream infection diagnosis can accurately predict 28-day all-cause mortality, outperforming traditional severity scores like APACHE II or SOFA.
proposals.method: We will analyze a multi-center cohort of adult patients with CRPA bacteremia, extracting structured data from electronic health records (EHRs) and microbiological reports from the first 72 hours post-culture. Features will include demographics, comorbidities (e.g., prior infections, transplant status), vital signs, laboratory values (e.g., WBC, creatinine), microbiological data (e.g., carbapenem MICs), and initial treatment details. We will train and compare several ML algorithms, including gradient boosting machines (XGBoost, LightGBM) and regularized logistic regression, using nested cross-validation to optimize hyperparameters and prevent overfitting. Model performance will be evaluated using the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC), calibration plots, and decision curve analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, interpretable ML tool for early mortality risk prediction in CRPA bacteremia, a high-stakes clinical scenario. It will identify the most critical predictive variables from the early infection course, informing both bedside clinical decision-making and the design of future interventional trials targeting high-risk subgroups. The model framework can be adapted for other multidrug-resistant gram-negative infections.
proposals.novelty: While ML has been applied to sepsis prediction broadly, this proposal focuses specifically on the high-mortality, niche population of CRPA bacteremia, integrating detailed prior infection history and early microbiological phenotypes—variables often missing from generic models—to create a pathogen- and resistance-specific prognostic tool.

proposals.title: Geospatial Analysis of Regional Efficacy in Infection Prevention Trials: A Case Study of Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Multidrug-Resistant Escherichia coli
proposals.motivation: Infection prevention interventions, such as decolonization protocols or environmental cleaning, are evaluated in multi-center trials, but their efficacy is often reported as an aggregate. Pre-2026 evidence suggests significant regional variation in pathogen prevalence, antimicrobial resistance patterns, and healthcare practices, which may obscure location-specific intervention effects. Understanding these geospatial heterogeneities is critical for optimizing the deployment of infection control resources and interpreting trial generalizability, particularly for high-risk pathogens like CRPA and MDR E. coli in vulnerable patient populations.
proposals.hypothesis: The clinical efficacy of infection prevention interventions, as measured by pathogen-specific infection rates and patient mortality, exhibits significant spatial clustering that correlates with regional differences in baseline resistance prevalence, healthcare facility characteristics, and prior antibiotic exposure among patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a secondary analysis of existing multi-center trial data (e.g., from prior studies on bedside wipes or stewardship) and surveillance data (e.g., CDC's NHSN, pre-2026). Patient-level outcomes and pathogen data will be geocoded to trial site locations. Using ArcGIS or R with the 'sf' and 'spdep' packages, we will perform spatial autocorrelation analysis (Global and Local Moran's I) to identify clusters of high or low intervention efficacy. We will then build hierarchical Bayesian spatial models (using INLA or CAR models) to quantify the association between regional covariates (e.g., local resistance rates from public health reports, nursing home density, prior flu incidence as a proxy for healthcare pressure) and site-level outcome differences, adjusting for patient-level risk factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for analyzing geographic heterogeneity in infection prevention trials, moving beyond 'one-size-fits-all' efficacy estimates. It will identify specific regions where interventions under- or over-perform, offering actionable intelligence for public health targeting. The methodology will also establish a precedent for incorporating geospatial analysis into the design and interpretation of future antimicrobial stewardship and infection control studies.
proposals.novelty: While geospatial methods are used in infectious disease epidemiology, their application to dissect efficacy within randomized controlled trials of infection prevention is novel. This approach integrates clinical trial outcomes with regional public health data to explain, rather than just describe, spatial variation in intervention success.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of a Composite Clinical Endpoint for Trials of Novel Antibiotics in Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia
proposals.motivation: Trials for hospital-acquired pneumonia (HAP) and ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP), especially those involving multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogens like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA), face significant challenges. Traditional primary endpoints, such as clinical cure at a single time point, often fail to capture the full patient-important impact of treatment, including mortality, sustained clinical response, and serious adverse events like Clostridioides difficile infection or acute kidney injury. This fragmented assessment complicates the interpretation of an antibiotic's net clinical benefit, a critical factor for clinicians, regulators, and antimicrobial stewardship programs.
proposals.hypothesis: A novel composite endpoint, integrating all-cause mortality, sustained clinical cure, and significant safety events into a single ordinal outcome, will provide a more comprehensive and patient-centered measure of treatment success for HAP/VAP trials than traditional endpoints used in isolation.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using data from prior HAP/VAP clinical trials and observational studies in high-risk populations (e.g., oncology, transplant patients). The composite endpoint will be constructed as an ordinal scale: 1) Death by test-of-cure (TOC); 2) Survival with a significant safety event (e.g., C. difficile infection, renal failure); 3) Survival without safety event but without clinical cure at TOC; 4) Survival without safety event with clinical cure at TOC. We will apply methods from cardiology trial analysis (e.g., win ratio, analysis of the composite using proportional odds models) to evaluate the endpoint's statistical properties, discriminant validity, and correlation with long-term outcomes like 28-day mortality.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a validated, standardized composite endpoint for future pneumonia trials, facilitating more efficient and clinically meaningful evaluation of novel antibiotics. It will directly inform clinical trial design and regulatory discussions by providing a framework to assess the net therapeutic benefit of agents targeting resistant gram-negative infections, ultimately supporting better treatment decisions and stewardship.
proposals.novelty: While composite endpoints are standard in cardiology, their application to infectious disease trials is nascent. This proposal innovatively adapts these methodologies specifically for pneumonia, explicitly balancing efficacy and safety to define treatment success, which is a non-obvious but critical advancement beyond the current siloed evaluation of cure and mortality.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Imipenem-Cilastatin-Relebactam (IMI/REL) for the Prevention of Hospital-Acquired and Ventilator-Associated Bacterial Pneumonia in High-Risk ICU Patients with Prior Carbapenem-Resistant Gram-Negative Colonization
proposals.motivation: Hospital-acquired and ventilator-associated bacterial pneumonia (HABP/VABP) caused by carbapenem-resistant organisms (CROs), such as carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Enterobacterales (CRE), is associated with high mortality, particularly in immunocompromised and critically ill patients. Prior colonization is a key risk factor for subsequent infection, yet effective prophylactic strategies for these high-risk patients are lacking. This study addresses the critical need for evidence-based, targeted prophylaxis to prevent lethal infections in a vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: In ICU patients with known prior rectal or respiratory colonization by carbapenem-resistant gram-negative bacteria, prophylactic administration of imipenem-cilastatin-relebactam (IMI/REL) will reduce the incidence of HABP/VABP caused by these pathogens compared to standard care (placebo).
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. High-risk ICU patients (e.g., post-hematopoietic stem cell transplant, oncology, prolonged ventilation) with prior confirmed colonization by CRPA or CRE will be randomized to receive either intravenous IMI/REL or placebo for a defined prophylactic course. The primary endpoint will be the incidence of microbiologically confirmed HABP/VABP caused by a carbapenem-resistant gram-negative pathogen within 30 days. Secondary endpoints include all-cause mortality, infection-related mortality, and the emergence of resistance. Statistical analysis will use an intention-to-treat approach with a time-to-event analysis for the primary outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence for the efficacy of a targeted, novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination as prophylaxis against lethal CRO infections. It will establish a potential new paradigm for infection prevention in the highest-risk patients, moving beyond reactive treatment to proactive, microbiologically-guided intervention. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and infection control practices in ICUs and immunocompromised patient units.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on infection risk factors and treatment, this study directly tests a novel agent for *prevention* in a precisely defined, colonized population, a strategy not yet evaluated in randomized trials. It leverages the specific activity of IMI/REL against prevalent resistant pathogens (CRPA, CRE) identified in the researcher's prior epidemiology work.

proposals.title: Clinical Validation of In Vitro Synergy Testing for Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Klebsiella pneumoniae Bloodstream Infections: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Infections caused by carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) are associated with high mortality, especially in immunocompromised hosts. Combination antibiotic therapy guided by in vitro synergy testing is often employed, but its clinical utility remains debated due to a lack of robust evidence linking laboratory results to patient outcomes. Validating this link is critical for antimicrobial stewardship and improving survival in these high-risk infections.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with CRPA or CRKP bloodstream infections, receipt of an antibiotic combination demonstrating in vitro synergy (via checkerboard or time-kill assay) will be independently associated with reduced 30-day all-cause mortality compared to patients receiving non-synergistic combinations or monotherapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRPA or CRKP bacteremia from 2020-2024. The primary exposure is treatment with a combination regimen tested for in vitro synergy. Clinical microbiology lab records will be used to obtain synergy test results (synergistic, indifferent, or antagonistic). The primary outcome is 30-day mortality. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for key confounders including Pitt bacteremia score, source of infection, prior antibiotic exposure, and underlying comorbidities (e.g., hematologic malignancy, transplant status), to assess the independent association between synergistic therapy and mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide one of the largest direct assessments of whether in vitro synergy translates to clinical benefit for high-priority resistant gram-negative infections. The findings will offer evidence-based guidance for antimicrobial stewardship programs and clinicians on the utility of routine synergy testing. It will also identify patient subgroups (e.g., stem cell transplant recipients) most likely to benefit from synergy-guided therapy.
proposals.novelty: While synergy testing is common, most studies are in vitro or small case series; this large, confounder-adjusted clinical outcomes study directly addresses a critical evidence gap. It leverages the investigator's expertise in resistant infections in vulnerable populations and uses robust, pre-2026 epidemiological methods to test a foundational assumption of infectious disease practice.

proposals.title: Long-term Mortality, Recurrence, and Microbiological Carriage in Survivors of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Bacteremia: A Multi-Center, Matched Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly carbapenem-resistant *Klebsiella pneumoniae* (CRKP) and *E. coli* (CREC), are a critical global threat associated with high short-term mortality. However, pre-2026 research, including Dr. Kaye's work, has focused primarily on acute infection management and immediate outcomes. The long-term clinical trajectory, risk of recurrent infection, and duration of gastrointestinal carriage in patients who survive the initial episode remain poorly characterized, creating a significant gap in understanding the full burden of CRE.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients who survive an initial CRE bloodstream infection experience significantly higher long-term all-cause mortality and recurrence rates compared to matched controls with susceptible infections or no infection, and a substantial proportion remain colonized with CRE for over a year, acting as a reservoir for transmission.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-center, matched cohort study. Adult survivors (discharged alive) of monomicrobial CRE bacteremia will be identified via clinical microbiology databases (2015-2024). Each case will be matched 1:1 with two control cohorts: patients with carbapenem-susceptible Enterobacterales bacteremia and hospitalized patients without Gram-negative bacteremia, matched on age, sex, comorbidity index, and admission date. Primary outcomes are 1-year and 3-year all-cause mortality, obtained via linkage to national death indices and electronic health records. Secondary outcomes include microbiologically-confirmed recurrence and duration of carriage, assessed by analyzing all subsequent clinical cultures. We will use Cox proportional hazards regression to compare mortality and recurrence risks, adjusting for additional confounders like prior antibiotic exposure and invasive devices.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive, longitudinal assessment of the post-discharge burden of CRE survivorship. It will quantify the excess long-term mortality and recurrence risk, informing long-term follow-up and surveillance strategies. Furthermore, by defining the natural history of carriage, it will critically inform infection prevention policies for decolonization and contact precautions in high-risk settings like nursing homes and oncology units.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have examined acute mortality, this proposal shifts focus to the understudied survivor population, employing a rigorous matched-cohort design to isolate the long-term impact of CRE infection itself from underlying patient frailty. It uniquely integrates long-term clinical outcomes with detailed longitudinal microbiological data to map the carriage reservoir.

proposals.title: Long-term Mortality, Healthcare Utilization, and Transmission Risk in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales Colonization versus Infection: A Multi-Center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are a critical public health threat. While CRE infections are known to increase mortality, the long-term clinical burden of asymptomatic CRE colonization is often considered minimal, potentially leading to under-prioritization of infection control measures for colonized patients. This study directly challenges that assumption by quantifying the long-term outcomes and transmission potential of colonization, which is crucial for informing stewardship and infection prevention policies, especially in high-risk settings like nursing homes and oncology units.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with CRE colonization, without a documented infection at baseline, will have a significantly higher risk of long-term all-cause mortality, subsequent CRE infection, and increased healthcare utilization compared to matched non-colonized controls, and will serve as a significant reservoir for onward transmission within healthcare facilities.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using electronic health record and microbiology data from 2018-2025. Adult patients with a first positive CRE clinical culture (infection cohort) or rectal surveillance swab (colonization cohort) will be identified. Each will be matched 1:2 to controls by age, sex, facility, and time-at-risk. Primary outcomes will be 1-year all-cause mortality and time to first CRE infection (for colonized patients). Secondary outcomes include hospital/ICU days, antibiotic days, and transmission events quantified using whole-genome sequencing of isolates from potential contacts. Analyses will use Cox proportional hazards and negative binomial regression models, adjusting for comorbidities (e.g., McCabe score), prior antibiotic use, and device exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, longitudinal evidence on the 'hidden' burden of CRE colonization, directly comparing it to infection. It will generate actionable data on the risk of progression from colonization to infection and the transmission contribution of colonized patients, filling a critical evidence gap. The findings will inform more nuanced infection prevention strategies and antimicrobial stewardship programs by highlighting the need to address the reservoir of colonized patients.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have focused on short-term outcomes or infection risk alone, this proposal integrates long-term clinical burden, transmission dynamics via genomic sequencing, and healthcare utilization into a single, matched-cohort design to holistically challenge the perceived unimportance of colonization. It leverages Dr. Kaye's expertise in resistant gram-negative epidemiology and infection prevention trials in a novel, high-impact synthesis.

proposals.title: A Multi-Setting, Population-Based Cohort Study of Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Colonization and Subsequent Clinical Outcomes in Long-Term Care Facilities and the Community
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are a critical public health threat, with most research and surveillance focused on acute care hospitals. However, long-term care facilities (LTCFs) and community settings are increasingly recognized as reservoirs for CRE, facilitating transmission and seeding hospital outbreaks. Understanding the epidemiology and outcomes of CRE in these under-studied settings is essential for designing effective, community-wide infection prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the prevalence of CRE colonization in LTCF residents and high-risk outpatients is substantial and independently associated with an increased risk of subsequent CRE infection, hospitalization, and all-cause mortality compared to non-colonized matched controls.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, multi-center cohort study across a network of LTCFs and affiliated outpatient clinics. A baseline point-prevalence survey using rectal swabs will screen for CRE colonization (using chromogenic agar and PCR for carbapenemase genes). Colonized individuals and matched non-colonized controls will be followed for 12 months. The primary outcome is the incidence of subsequent CRE infection (clinical culture). Secondary outcomes include all-cause hospitalization and mortality. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to model the independent risk of colonization on outcomes, adjusting for demographics, comorbidities, prior healthcare exposure, and antibiotic use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, population-based estimates of CRE burden and attributable risk in non-acute care settings. It will quantify the clinical impact of colonization outside hospitals, informing targeted screening and stewardship programs. The findings will shift the paradigm from hospital-centric to community-wide CRE control.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described CRE in single settings (e.g., nursing homes), this study integrates LTCF and outpatient data to model the continuum of care. It moves beyond prevalence to directly measure the longitudinal risk of infection and mortality attributable to colonization in these populations, a critical evidence gap pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Micro-Costing Analysis of Healthcare Resource Utilization for Infections Caused by Carbapenemase-Producing vs. Non-Carbapenemase-Producing Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE)
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are a critical public health threat associated with high mortality and resource use. However, CRE is a heterogeneous group; infections caused by carbapenemase-producing (CP) strains, particularly KPC or NDM, may require more complex isolation protocols, specialized diagnostics, and last-line antibiotics like ceftazidime-avibactam compared to non-CP-CRE. Current bundled reimbursement models and stewardship interventions often treat all CRE infections uniformly, potentially misallocating resources and underestimating the true cost burden of the most resistant subsets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the direct medical costs of managing CRE infections are significantly higher for CP-CRE compared to non-CP-CRE, driven by differences in antibiotic costs, length of stay, and infection control measures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective micro-costing analysis from the hospital payer perspective. Using existing electronic health record and microbiology data (2018-2024) from a tertiary care center, we will identify adult patients with clinically significant CRE infections (e.g., bloodstream, urinary tract). Isolates will be categorized by molecular subset (CP-CRE vs. non-CP-CRE) via existing PCR or whole-genome sequencing data. A time-driven activity-based costing (TDABC) framework will be applied to quantify resources: antibiotic days (with drug-specific acquisition costs), isolation days, diagnostic tests (e.g., repeat cultures, susceptibility testing), and attributable length of stay. Multivariable generalized linear models will compare total costs between groups, adjusting for confounders like infection source and severity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed, molecular subtype-specific cost analysis for CRE management, quantifying the financial burden gradient within this pathogen class. The results will inform more accurate economic models for antimicrobial stewardship programs and provide data to advocate for refined reimbursement policies that reflect the variable complexity of resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have compared outcomes like mortality between CRE subsets, a formal micro-costing analysis differentiating CP-CRE from non-CP-CRE has not been performed. This moves beyond aggregate 'CRE' cost estimates to a precision public health economics approach, aligning with the growing recognition of heterogeneity within antibiotic-resistant pathogens.

proposals.title: Risk-Stratified vs. Universal Imipenem-Cilastatin Background Therapy: A Retrospective Cohort Study on Mortality and Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) Acquisition in High-Risk Hematology-Oncology Patients
proposals.motivation: Empiric broad-spectrum carbapenems like imipenem-cilastatin are commonly used as background therapy for febrile neutropenia in high-risk hematology-oncology patients, driven by concerns over mortality from resistant gram-negative infections. However, universal use may unnecessarily drive the selection and spread of carbapenem-resistant organisms, particularly CRPA, a major threat in this vulnerable population. A risk-stratified approach could preserve efficacy while mitigating resistance, but evidence to challenge the universal assumption is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: Among high-risk hematology-oncology patients, a risk-stratified approach to background imipenem-cilastatin therapy, based on prior colonization and infection history, will be non-inferior for all-cause 30-day mortality and will be associated with a lower incidence of subsequent CRPA acquisition compared to a universal therapy approach.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-center cohort study of adult patients undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplant or intensive chemotherapy who received imipenem-cilastatin as empiric background therapy. Using electronic health record data, we will apply a multivariable logistic regression model to identify independent risk factors for gram-negative bacteremia and mortality. These factors will be used to create a clinical risk score. We will then compare outcomes between a simulated 'risk-stratified' cohort (therapy only for high-risk patients) and the observed 'universal' cohort using propensity score matching and competing risk regression analysis for CRPA acquisition.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative risk score to guide imipenem-cilastatin use in this setting, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship. It will generate crucial evidence on the safety of de-escalating universal carbapenem coverage and its potential impact on reducing CRPA, a key resistant pathogen. The findings will offer a practical framework for balancing individual patient risk with the collective need to curb resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described CRPA epidemiology and stewardship challenges, this study directly tests the necessity of universal background therapy by developing and validating a patient-specific risk-stratification tool, moving beyond descriptive analyses to a actionable, data-driven intervention strategy.

proposals.title: Risk Factors for Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) Colonization and Subsequent Infection-Related Mortality in Pediatric Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplant Recipients: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Pediatric and immunocompromised patients, particularly those undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT), are at extreme risk for infections with multidrug-resistant organisms like CRPA, which carry high mortality. Prior studies in adults and general oncology populations have identified risk factors, but data specific to the high-risk pediatric HSCT population, where immune reconstitution and antibiotic exposure patterns differ, are scarce. This gap hinders the development of targeted antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention strategies for this vulnerable group.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prior exposure to specific antibiotic classes (e.g., carbapenems, anti-pseudomonal cephalosporins) and prolonged neutropenia are independent risk factors for CRPA colonization in pediatric HSCT patients, and that CRPA colonization is a significant predictor of infection-related mortality within 90 days post-transplant.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of pediatric patients (<18 years) who underwent allogeneic HSCT between 2015-2025. The primary exposure is CRPA colonization, identified via routine surveillance rectal swabs. Using electronic health record data, we will employ multivariable logistic regression to model risk factors for colonization. For the mortality outcome, we will use a Cox proportional hazards model, adjusting for transplant-related variables. Specific algorithms will include propensity score matching to control for confounding by indication in antibiotic exposure analyses and competing risk analysis for mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dedicated risk model for CRPA in pediatric HSCT, directly informing pre-emptive isolation and stewardship decisions. It will quantify the mortality burden attributable to CRPA colonization in this population, offering crucial data for clinical trial design. The findings will extend Dr. Kaye's prior work on resistant gram-negative bacteria and stewardship in oncology to a critically ill pediatric cohort.
proposals.novelty: While CRPA epidemiology has been studied in adult wards and nursing homes, this proposal uniquely focuses on the intersection of pediatric age, profound immunosuppression from HSCT, and the specific sequence of antibiotic exposures preceding transplantation. It moves beyond describing incidence to modeling colonization as a dynamic risk state predictive of mortality.

proposals.title: A Clinical Prediction Model to Differentiate Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) Infection from Colonization and Its Association with 30-Day Mortality
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) is a critical public health threat associated with high mortality. However, positive CRPA cultures can represent either true infection or asymptomatic colonization, leading to misclassification in outcome studies and potentially obscuring the true mortality burden of infection. Refining outcome analysis by accurately distinguishing these states is essential for benchmarking hospital performance, designing targeted interventions, and improving antimicrobial stewardship, particularly in high-risk populations like oncology and transplant patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a clinical prediction model incorporating microbiological, clinical, and laboratory data can reliably differentiate CRPA infection from colonization, and that true CRPA infection, but not colonization, will be independently associated with increased 30-day all-cause mortality after adjusting for confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with a positive CRPA culture (clinical or surveillance) from 2018-2024. Using pre-2026 consensus definitions (e.g., CDC/NHSN criteria adapted with expert adjudication), each episode will be classified as infection or colonization. We will develop a multivariable logistic regression model with LASSO regularization for variable selection to predict infection status, incorporating features like systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) criteria, site of culture, leukocyte count, prior antibiotic exposure, and immunosuppression. The model's performance will be assessed via cross-validation. Subsequently, a Cox proportional hazards model will evaluate the independent association of adjudicated infection status with 30-day mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, pragmatic tool to improve the accuracy of CRPA outcome surveillance and research by reducing misclassification bias. It will deliver a more precise estimate of the mortality attributable to CRPA infection itself, separate from the underlying patient frailty often associated with colonization. This will inform hospital infection prevention priorities and stewardship programs by clarifying which patients with CRPA positivity are at the highest risk of death.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has described CRPA epidemiology, this study moves beyond reporting crude culture rates to explicitly model the differentiation of infection from colonization and directly link this refined phenotype to mortality risk. The application of a formal prediction model and mortality analysis specifically for CRPA, integrating the researcher's expertise in resistant infections and patient outcomes, addresses a key methodological gap in the field.

proposals.title: Carbapenemase Type and 30-Day All-Cause Mortality: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study of Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterobacterales Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant organisms (CROs), particularly carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Enterobacterales (CRE), are a critical global health threat associated with high mortality. However, prior studies often aggregate all carbapenemase-producing organisms (CPOs) or focus on specific species, obscuring potential differences in virulence and mortality risk conferred by specific carbapenemase enzymes (e.g., KPC, NDM, VIM, OXA-48-like). Understanding these differential impacts is crucial for refining risk stratification and informing targeted infection control and therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among patients with CRPA or CRE bloodstream or lower respiratory tract infections, the specific carbapenemase type (e.g., KPC, NDM, VIM, OXA-48-like) is an independent predictor of 30-day all-cause mortality, with KPC and NDM associated with the highest risk.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRPA or CRE infections from 2020-2025. The primary exposure is carbapenemase type, confirmed via PCR or whole-genome sequencing. The primary outcome is 30-day all-cause mortality. We will use multivariable logistic regression, adjusting for key confounders identified from prior work (e.g., age, comorbidities, severity of illness, source of infection, and prior antibiotic exposure). Subgroup analyses will be performed for species (e.g., CRPA vs. CRE) and infection source. Model performance will be assessed using the c-statistic and calibration plots.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, comparative analysis of mortality risk stratified by carbapenemase type across two major CRO groups. It will generate evidence to move beyond a binary 'CPO vs. non-CPO' classification, offering a more nuanced risk profile for clinicians. The findings could directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs and infection prevention policies by highlighting the highest-risk resistance determinants.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies by this researcher and others have examined epidemiology and general risk factors for resistant infections, this proposal uniquely focuses on dissecting the clinical impact of specific carbapenemase enzymes themselves, a granularity not commonly addressed in large clinical outcome studies pre-2026.

proposals.title: Protective Factors and Clinical Trajectories Associated with Survival from Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Bacteremia: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) infections are associated with high mortality, and research has predominantly focused on identifying risk factors for poor outcomes. This deficit limits the development of proactive, patient-centered management strategies. An inverse problem approach, identifying factors associated with survival, could reveal modifiable protective practices and inform targeted stewardship and supportive care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific modifiable factors—including early source control, appropriate initial combination antimicrobial therapy, and host immune status markers—are independently associated with survival from CRPA bloodstream infection, even after adjusting for known mortality risks.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRPA bacteremia from 2020-2025. The primary outcome is 30-day survival. Using pre-2026 methods, we will employ multivariable logistic regression to identify protective factors. To address confounding by indication and model complex interactions, we will also utilize propensity score matching (for treatment variables) and classification and regression tree (CART) analysis to define high-survival patient subgroups and their characteristic clinical trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, actionable framework for managing CRPA infections by shifting the focus from risk to resilience. It will generate specific hypotheses for future interventional studies aimed at enhancing protective factors. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and supportive care protocols in high-risk settings like oncology and transplant units.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye and others has detailed mortality risks, this study explicitly inverts the analytical framework to model survival. It integrates advanced, pre-2026 causal inference methods (propensity scoring) with machine learning (CART) not commonly applied in this context to uncover non-linear, synergistic protective pathways.

proposals.title: Long-term Mortality and Clinical Failure at Day 28 in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Klebsiella pneumoniae Bloodstream Infections: A Multi-center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Prior studies of antimicrobial-resistant (AMR) gram-negative infections, particularly in high-risk populations like oncology and nursing home patients, often assess short-term outcomes (e.g., 14-day mortality). However, emerging pre-2026 evidence suggests that the clinical and physiological sequelae of these infections, including treatment failure and relapse, may drive significant mortality and morbidity beyond this acute window. A comprehensive understanding of long-term outcomes is critical for accurate prognostication and for evaluating the true impact of infection control and stewardship interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among patients with carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) or carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) bloodstream infections, all-cause mortality and clinical failure rates at day 28 will be significantly higher than at day 14, and that prior healthcare exposure and specific comorbidities will be independent risk factors for late failure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multi-center cohort study of adult patients with CRPA or CRKP bacteremia from 2018-2024. The primary outcome is all-cause mortality at day 28; secondary outcomes include composite clinical failure (mortality, persistent bacteremia, or recurrence) at day 28. Using pre-existing electronic health record data, we will apply multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to identify risk factors for 28-day mortality, adjusting for demographics, Charlson Comorbidity Index, severity of illness (e.g., Pitt bacteremia score), source of infection, and prior antibiotic/healthcare exposure (e.g., recent hospitalization, nursing home residence). We will compare Kaplan-Meier survival curves for day 14 versus day 28.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, long-term outcome data for CRPA/CRKP bacteremia, filling a key evidence gap. It will quantify the burden of late mortality and treatment failure, informing patient and family counseling. The identified risk factors will help clinicians stratify patients who may benefit from more intensive long-term monitoring or novel antimicrobial strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by this researcher and others has focused on acute infection outcomes or specific settings (nursing homes, oncology), this proposal uniquely shifts the focus to the understudied 28-day horizon for CRPA/CRKP, directly linking prior healthcare exposure to long-term survival in a multi-center design that enhances generalizability.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Respiratory Microbiome Dynamics and the Emergence of Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa in High-Risk Hematology Patients
proposals.motivation: Antibiotic use in high-risk patients, like those undergoing hematopoietic stem cell transplant, disrupts the respiratory microbiome, potentially creating an ecological niche for resistant pathogens. Pre-2026, studies have linked gut dysbiosis to infection risk, but the longitudinal dynamics of the respiratory microbiome and its direct role in facilitating colonization and infection with pathogens like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) remain poorly characterized. This gap limits our ability to predict and prevent lethal infections in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, antibiotic-induced perturbations in the longitudinal respiratory microbiome ecology, characterized by loss of diversity and key commensals, precede and predict the acquisition and clinical emergence of CRPA in hematology patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of hematology patients at high risk for CRPA. Serial oropharyngeal/nasal swabs will be collected weekly from admission until discharge. DNA will be extracted for 16S rRNA gene sequencing (V4 region) using Illumina MiSeq. Bioinformatic analysis will employ QIIME2 for processing, DADA2 for sequence variant inference, and the R packages 'phyloseq' and 'vegan' for longitudinal alpha/beta-diversity analysis. We will use mixed-effects models and machine learning (LASSO regression) to identify microbiome features (e.g., specific taxa, diversity indices) associated with subsequent CRPA isolation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal map of respiratory microbiome ecology in relation to antibiotic exposures and CRPA outcomes in hematology patients. It will identify specific microbial signatures that serve as early biomarkers for resistance emergence, moving beyond descriptive epidemiology to mechanistic ecology. The findings will directly inform targeted antimicrobial stewardship and microbiome-based preventive strategies to reduce mortality from resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye et al. has focused on the epidemiology of resistance and infection control interventions, this proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal, high-resolution microbiome ecology into the study of resistance in a high-risk clinical population, applying ecological theory to a pressing clinical problem.

proposals.title: Feasibility and Clinical Outcomes of Oral Step-down Therapy Following Short-Course Intravenous Cefiderocol for Resistant Gram-Negative Infections: A Multi-center Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Cefiderocol is a critical last-line agent for carbapenem-resistant gram-negative infections, but prolonged intravenous therapy burdens healthcare systems and patients. Prior work by Kaye et al. highlights the high mortality and infection risks in vulnerable populations like nursing home residents and oncology patients. Defining safe, effective oral step-down strategies after initial intravenous cefiderocol could reduce length of stay, costs, and complications of long-term IV access, addressing a key gap in antimicrobial stewardship for highly resistant pathogens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transition to an oral antibiotic regimen (e.g., high-dose oral fosfomycin or a fluoroquinolone, based on susceptibility) after 3-5 days of intravenous cefiderocol will be non-inferior to a standard 10-14 day full intravenous course in terms of clinical cure and 30-day mortality for select infections.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using patient data from 2019-2025. The cohort will include adult inpatients treated with IV cefiderocol for ≥48 hours for infections caused by carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA), Acinetobacter baumannii, or Enterobacterales. Using propensity score matching (PSM) based on age, infection source, severity, and pathogen, we will compare outcomes between patients who received oral step-down after ≤7 days of IV therapy and those who completed a full IV course. Primary outcomes are 30-day all-cause mortality and clinical cure at end-of-therapy. Secondary outcomes include readmission, recurrent infection, and Clostridioides difficile infection. Multivariable logistic regression will adjust for residual confounding.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first real-world evidence on the feasibility and outcomes of an oral step-down strategy following cefiderocol, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship protocols. It will generate data to support early hospital discharge and outpatient management for resistant infections, potentially reducing healthcare costs and patient exposure to IV line-associated risks. The findings will offer a practical framework for de-escalation in an era of limited novel antibiotics.
proposals.novelty: While oral step-down is common for many infections, its application following a siderophore cephalosporin like cefiderocol is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates Kaye's expertise in resistant infections in high-risk settings with a pragmatic stewardship approach focused on reducing IV antibiotic duration, moving beyond the traditional paradigm of prolonged intravenous therapy for these pathogens.

proposals.title: A Matched Cohort Analysis of Mortality and Prior Healthcare Exposure in Patients with Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Bloodstream and Respiratory Infections
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) represent a critical public health threat, with high associated mortality. Prior studies, including Dr. Kaye's work on CRPA, have established the utility of matched outcomes methodology for isolating the impact of resistance, but its application to CRE across diverse infection sites is limited. Understanding the site-specific mortality burden and the role of prior healthcare exposures (e.g., recent hospitalization, nursing home residence) is essential for targeted prevention in high-risk populations.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients with CRE bloodstream or respiratory infections have significantly higher 30-day all-cause mortality compared to propensity score-matched patients with carbapenem-susceptible infections, and this excess mortality risk is modified by specific prior healthcare exposures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, multicenter cohort study using data from 2018-2024. The primary exposure is a laboratory-confirmed CRE (E. coli or Klebsiella spp.) infection from blood or respiratory culture. For each case, we will identify a control with a carbapenem-susceptible infection at the same site, matched 1:1 using propensity scores based on age, comorbidities (Charlson Index), severity of illness (e.g., ICU admission, SOFA score), and infection source. The primary outcome is 30-day mortality, analyzed using conditional logistic regression. Secondary analyses will evaluate effect modification by prior hospitalization, nursing home residence, and prior antibiotic use.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide robust, site-specific estimates of the mortality burden attributable to CRE, extending beyond prior surgical site infection analyses. It will identify key modifiable risk factors in the patient's prior healthcare journey, informing stewardship interventions and infection prevention protocols for hematology-oncology and other high-risk units.
proposals.novelty: While matched analyses exist for specific pathogens like CRPA, this study applies the rigorous methodology to CRE across critical infection sites, explicitly integrating and testing prior healthcare exposures as effect modifiers rather than just confounders.

proposals.title: A Cost-Avoidance Model for Surgical Site Infection Prevention Programs: Integrating Attributable Costs and Resistant Pathogen Risk
proposals.motivation: Surgical site infections (SSIs), particularly those caused by resistant gram-negative bacteria like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Escherichia coli, significantly increase patient mortality, length of stay, and healthcare costs. In the pre-2026 era of value-based purchasing and bundled payments, hospitals urgently need robust, data-driven models to justify investments in infection prevention and antimicrobial stewardship programs. This is especially critical for high-risk populations, such as oncology and transplant patients identified in Dr. Kaye's prior work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a stochastic model incorporating procedure-specific SSI rates, attributable costs stratified by pathogen and resistance profile, and local antibiotic resistance epidemiology will demonstrate significant, quantifiable cost-avoidance from SSI prevention bundles, with the greatest financial benefit seen in preventing infections caused by resistant organisms.
proposals.method: We will construct a discrete-event simulation model using TreeAge Pro or R. The model will integrate inputs from: 1) local SSI surveillance data (by procedure), 2) a meta-analysis of pre-2026 literature to derive attributable costs for SSIs caused by susceptible vs. resistant gram-negative and gram-positive pathogens, and 3) local antibiogram data (e.g., CRPA prevalence). We will model the impact of a multi-modal prevention program (e.g., chlorhexidine bathing, antimicrobial stewardship) by applying relative risk reductions from published trials, like the bedside wipes study, to the baseline infection risk. A probabilistic sensitivity analysis will be performed using Monte Carlo simulation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide hospital administrators and infection prevention teams with a customizable, transparent model to forecast the return on investment for SSI prevention. It will explicitly quantify the added financial burden of antimicrobial resistance in surgical outcomes, strengthening the economic argument for stewardship. The model framework can be adapted to other healthcare-associated infections.
proposals.novelty: While cost-of-illness studies exist, this model uniquely integrates Dr. Kaye's expertise in resistant gram-negative epidemiology and infection control interventions to create a dynamic, pathogen-sensitive cost-avoidance tool, moving beyond simple per-infection cost estimates to a program-level financial analysis.

proposals.title: Real-World Effectiveness of Novel Antibiotics for Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections: A Pragmatic, Susceptibility-Inclusive Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Clinical trials for novel antibiotics against resistant Gram-negative pathogens like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) often require microbiological confirmation of susceptibility before enrollment, creating an efficacy-efficacy population that may not reflect real-world practice. This delays treatment and excludes patients who receive empiric therapy, biasing estimates of real-world mortality and clinical success. Understanding the effectiveness of newer agents (e.g., cefiderocol, ceftolozane-tazobactam) when used empirically is critical for antimicrobial stewardship and infection control, especially in high-risk populations such as oncology and nursing home patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the clinical failure and 30-day all-cause mortality rates for patients empirically treated with novel anti-pseudomonal agents for suspected CRPA infections will be non-inferior to those observed in the matched cohort of patients enrolled after susceptibility confirmation, after adjusting for key confounders including prior antibiotic exposure and infection source.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using a pragmatic design. Patients receiving a novel anti-pseudomonal agent for a suspected or confirmed CRPA infection will be identified from electronic health records and microbiology databases. The cohort will be divided into 'Empiric' (treatment initiated before susceptibility results) and 'Directed' (treatment initiated after results) groups. We will use propensity score matching based on demographics, comorbidities (e.g., neutropenia), infection source, and severity to control for confounding. The primary outcome is a composite of 30-day mortality or clinical failure (escalation/recurrence). Secondary outcomes include length of stay and resistance emergence. Statistical analysis will employ Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial real-world evidence on the effectiveness of newer antibiotics when used empirically, informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines and trial design. It will directly address the challenge of balancing rapid treatment initiation with optimal antibiotic selection in high-risk patients, a gap highlighted in prior stewardship literature. The findings will offer practical data for clinicians managing infections in settings like nursing homes and transplant units where resistant pathogens are prevalent.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel because it intentionally relaxes the standard microbiological enrollment criterion used in most post-marketing studies of novel antibiotics, creating a pragmatic cohort that mirrors actual clinical decision-making. It leverages advanced observational methods (propensity score matching) to compare empiric vs. directed therapy outcomes for CRPA, a question not addressed by traditional randomized controlled trials or restrictive cohort studies.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Safety of Ceftazidime-Avibactam for the Treatment of Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections in Non-Critically Ill Hospitalized Patients: A Multicenter, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Ceftazidime-avibactam (CAZ-AVI) is a critical agent for treating carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) infections, but its efficacy and safety data are largely derived from critically ill patients in intensive care settings. The risk-benefit profile and mortality impact of CAZ-AVI in less severely ill hospitalized patients, who constitute a large and growing population due to the spread of CRPA into community and nursing home settings, remain poorly characterized. Optimizing its use in this population is essential for antimicrobial stewardship and preventing the overuse of last-resort agents like colistin.
proposals.hypothesis: In non-critically ill hospitalized patients with CRPA infections, treatment with CAZ-AVI will be associated with lower 30-day all-cause mortality and a reduced incidence of acute kidney injury compared to treatment with colistin-based regimens, while demonstrating non-inferior clinical cure rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult, non-ICU hospitalized patients with CRPA infections (e.g., complicated urinary tract infection, pneumonia) from 2020-2025. Patients treated with CAZ-AVI will be compared to a propensity-score matched cohort treated with colistin-based therapy. The primary outcome is 30-day all-cause mortality. Secondary outcomes include clinical cure, microbiological eradication, length of stay, and nephrotoxicity. We will use multivariable logistic regression and Cox proportional hazards models, adjusting for confounders like prior antibiotic exposure, comorbidities (using the Charlson Comorbidity Index), and source of infection.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first dedicated evidence on the effectiveness and safety of CAZ-AVI in a non-critically ill inpatient population, filling a significant evidence gap. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship guidelines by defining an optimal patient population for CAZ-AVI use, potentially reducing reliance on toxic alternatives like colistin and preserving broader-spectrum agents for more severe cases.
proposals.novelty: This work inverts the typical study population for novel anti-CRPA agents from the critically ill to the moderately ill, a strategically important but understudied group where stewardship gains may be greatest. It leverages Dr. Kaye's expertise in resistant gram-negative epidemiology and crossover trial design within a novel, risk-stratified cohort framework.

proposals.title: Long-term Mortality and Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) Emergence Following Treatment for Gram-Negative Bacteremia in High-Risk Oncology Patients
proposals.motivation: Patients with hematologic malignancies and stem cell transplants are at extreme risk for infections with resistant gram-negative bacteria like CRPA, leading to high initial mortality. However, pre-2026 studies often have short follow-up, missing the long-term survival deficit and the risk of subsequent resistant infections after initial treatment, which are critical for understanding the full burden of antimicrobial resistance in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oncology patients surviving an initial episode of gram-negative bacteremia have significantly elevated one-year all-cause mortality compared to matched controls, and that a substantial proportion of survivors will develop subsequent infections with resistant organisms, particularly CRPA, within six months of treatment completion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective, matched cohort study using electronic health records from a comprehensive cancer center (2018-2024). Adult patients with hematologic malignancies or stem cell transplants and a positive blood culture for Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Enterobacterales will be identified. Cases will be matched 1:2 to oncology controls without bacteremia on age, malignancy, transplant status, and time since diagnosis. Survival will be analyzed using Kaplan-Meier curves and Cox proportional hazards models. The emergence of resistance will be defined as a subsequent clinical culture with a carbapenem-resistant isolate (e.g., CRPA, CRE) within 180 days of completing antibiotics for the index bacteremia, assessed via multivariable logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This study will quantify the long-term survival penalty attributable to gram-negative bacteremia in oncology patients, moving beyond 30-day mortality. It will also provide novel data on the risk of 'collateral damage'—the subsequent emergence of highly resistant pathogens following antibiotic treatment—informing long-term surveillance and stewardship strategies for survivors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye et al. has focused on epidemiology and acute outcomes, this proposal uniquely links an initial infection episode to long-term survival and specifically tracks the downstream emergence of resistance in a longitudinal patient-centered framework, which is not standard in pre-2026 antimicrobial resistance research.

proposals.title: Ecological Shifts in the Gut Microbiome as a Mediator of Mortality Risk Following Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infection in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Prior antibiotic exposure and resistant infections like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) are major drivers of mortality in vulnerable populations, including oncology and nursing home patients. Pre-2026 research shows the gut microbiome influences infection risk and immune recovery, but the specific ecological disruption caused by CRPA treatment and its direct link to patient survival remains poorly characterized. Understanding this link is critical for developing microbiome-informed stewardship strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treatment for CRPA infection causes a severe, persistent ecological disruption (dysbiosis) in the patient's gut microbiome, characterized by loss of diversity and key commensal taxa, and that the magnitude of this disruption is an independent predictor of 90-day all-cause mortality, mediating the risk associated with prior antibiotic exposure.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of hospitalized patients with CRPA infection, collecting serial stool and respiratory samples pre-, during, and post-treatment. Microbial DNA will be sequenced (16S rRNA gene V4 region). We will quantify dysbiosis using alpha-diversity (Shannon index) and beta-diversity (Bray-Curtis dissimilarity) metrics. The association between microbiome trajectories and mortality will be analyzed using joint longitudinal-survival models (e.g., time-dependent Cox models with microbiome principal components). Mediation analysis will test if microbiome metrics explain the effect of prior antibiotic exposure on mortality.
proposals.contribution: This study will define the specific microbiome signature of poor outcomes after CRPA infection, moving beyond correlation to mediation. It will provide a quantitative, ecological metric for stratifying patient risk in real-time. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship by highlighting the 'collateral damage' of treatment regimens, supporting the future design of microbiome-sparing protocols or adjunctive probiotics for high-risk groups.
proposals.novelty: While microbiome studies in infection are common, applying formal ecological metrics and mediation analysis from community ecology to directly link treatment-induced dysbiosis to mortality in CRPA patients is novel. This approach moves from describing association to modeling the microbiome as a mechanistic pathway, which is not standard in pre-2026 clinical studies of resistant infections.

proposals.title: Impact of Rapid Molecular Diagnostics on Mortality and Antimicrobial Use in High-Risk Patients with Suspected Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Escherichia coli Infections
proposals.motivation: Patients with prior healthcare exposures, such as those in nursing homes or oncology units, are at high risk for infections with resistant gram-negative pathogens like CRPA and CRE. Delays in identifying these pathogens and their resistance mechanisms lead to inappropriate empiric therapy, which is associated with increased mortality and broad-spectrum antibiotic overuse. Pre-2026, while rapid molecular diagnostics exist, their real-world impact on patient-centered outcomes in these high-risk, non-outbreak settings is not fully established.
proposals.hypothesis: The implementation of a point-of-care rapid molecular diagnostic panel for suspected gram-negative bloodstream infections, compared to standard culture-based methods, will reduce 30-day all-cause mortality and decrease time to effective and optimal antibiotic therapy in high-risk patients with a history of prior resistant infections or healthcare contact.
proposals.method: We propose a pragmatic, cluster-randomized crossover trial on hematology-oncology and transplant units. Clusters (hospital wards) will be randomized to either an intervention arm (using the BioFire® FilmArray® Blood Culture Identification 2 panel or the Cepheid Xpert® Carba-R assay on positive blood cultures) or a control arm (standard microbiology). The primary outcome is 30-day all-cause mortality. Secondary outcomes include time to effective therapy, duration of broad-spectrum antibiotic use (e.g., carbapenems), and hospital length of stay. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models to account for clustering and crossover periods.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide high-level evidence on whether rapid diagnostics directly improve survival in a targeted, high-risk population, moving beyond laboratory metrics. It will generate actionable data on antimicrobial stewardship opportunities by quantifying reductions in broad-spectrum antibiotic days. The findings will inform clinical guidelines and cost-benefit analyses for diagnostic implementation in settings with endemic resistant gram-negative bacteria.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have shown rapid diagnostics reduce time to results, this trial specifically targets a defined high-risk population with a prior history of resistant infections, evaluating a hard clinical outcome (mortality) in a non-outbreak, endemic setting. The crossover design within real-world stewardship programs assesses integration into routine care, not just efficacy under ideal conditions.

proposals.title: Efficacy and Resistance Risk of Dual Antibacterial Prophylaxis with Cefiderocol and Ceftazidime-Avibactam in High-Risk Hematology-Oncology Patients: A Multi-Center, Randomized, Controlled Trial
proposals.motivation: Patients with hematologic malignancies and profound neutropenia face high mortality from invasive infections caused by multidrug-resistant (MDR) gram-negative bacteria (GNB), particularly carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Enterobacterales (CRE). Existing fluoroquinolone prophylaxis is ineffective against these resistant pathogens and may itself drive resistance. There is an urgent need to evaluate novel, targeted prophylactic regimens that can prevent lethal breakthrough infections in this vulnerable population.
proposals.hypothesis: Prophylaxis with a combination of cefiderocol (a novel siderophore cephalosporin) and ceftazidime-avibactam will significantly reduce the incidence of invasive MDR GNB infections and infection-related mortality in high-risk neutropenic patients, compared to standard-of-care (no prophylaxis or fluoroquinolone prophylaxis), without leading to an unacceptable increase in subsequent colonization or infection with extensively drug-resistant organisms.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, open-label, randomized controlled trial with three arms: (1) dual prophylaxis (cefiderocol + ceftazidime-avibactam), (2) levofloxacin prophylaxis (active comparator), and (3) no prophylaxis/standard-of-care. High-risk adult patients (e.g., undergoing allogeneic HSCT or intensive chemotherapy for acute leukemia) will be enrolled at the onset of neutropenia. The primary outcome is the incidence of microbiologically confirmed invasive infection by MDR GNB (CRPA, CRE) during the neutropenic period. Secondary outcomes include infection-related mortality, overall survival, incidence of Clostridioides difficile infection, and emergence of resistance (measured via serial rectal swabs analyzed for carbapenemase genes and minimum inhibitory concentration shifts). Statistical analysis will use time-to-event methods (Kaplan-Meier, Cox regression) adjusting for center and prior colonization status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence on the clinical efficacy and ecological safety of using next-generation beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations as targeted prophylaxis. It will define the risk-benefit profile of a potent dual regimen, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship guidelines for the highest-risk immunocompromised hosts. The results will shape future strategies for infection prevention in the era of pan-drug resistance.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have evaluated fluoroquinolone prophylaxis, this is the first to test a proactive, dual-agent regimen specifically targeting the most concerning MDR pathogens (CRPA, CRE) with two of the newest, most potent agents. It uniquely integrates robust clinical efficacy endpoints with intensive microbiological surveillance to simultaneously assess prevention and resistance risk.

proposals.title: Predictors of Treatment Failure in Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa and Enterobacterales Bloodstream Infections: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Infections caused by carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) are associated with high mortality, but outcomes vary significantly between patients. Prior studies often focus on pathogen susceptibility, yet patient-specific factors and prior exposures may be critical determinants of treatment failure. Defining a composite profile of modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors is essential for guiding empirical therapy and stewardship in high-risk populations, such as oncology and transplant patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that treatment failure (a composite of 30-day mortality, persistent bacteremia, or recurrence) is independently associated with a combination of specific prior antibiotic exposures (e.g., recent fluoroquinolone or carbapenem use), host factors (e.g., neutropenia, high SOFA score), and pathogen genomic markers beyond the presence of a carbapenemase gene.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRPA or CRE bloodstream infections from 2020-2024. The primary outcome is treatment failure. Predictor variables will include detailed prior antibiotic history, clinical parameters, and whole-genome sequencing data for key resistance determinants and virulence factors. We will employ multivariable logistic regression for initial modeling and will train and validate a random forest classifier to identify non-linear interactions and rank variable importance for predicting the composite outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a clinically actionable risk stratification model for CRPA/CRE bloodstream infection outcomes, moving beyond simple susceptibility reporting. It will identify high-risk patient profiles to target for aggressive early intervention and inform stewardship by clarifying the impact of specific prior antibiotic exposures. The integration of clinical and genomic data aims to explain outcome heterogeneity.
proposals.novelty: While prior risk factor studies exist, this work uniquely applies an 'inverse problem' framework—defining predictors from a clear failure outcome—and integrates detailed prior exposure history with advanced pathogen genomics in a multi-center cohort of highly resistant infections, focusing on the understudied interface of oncology and antimicrobial resistance.

proposals.title: Determinants of Central Venous Catheter Preservation: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study of Patient, Device, and Care Factors Associated with Long-Term, Infection-Free Use
proposals.motivation: Central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) are a major cause of morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs, particularly in vulnerable populations like oncology and transplant patients. Research has overwhelmingly focused on risk factors for infection, creating a deficit in understanding the positive, protective factors that enable successful long-term line use. Identifying these factors is crucial for developing proactive, evidence-based strategies to preserve vascular access and improve patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, modifiable factors related to patient characteristics (e.g., immune status, prior antibiotic exposure), device attributes (e.g., type, insertion site), and care processes (e.g., maintenance protocols, stewardship practices) are independently associated with prolonged central venous catheter dwell time without infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with long-term central lines (e.g., PICCs, tunneled catheters, ports). Using electronic health record data, we will identify a cohort of 'successful preservation' (lines in place >90 days without infection) and a comparator cohort with early failure (infection or removal <90 days). We will employ multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression to model time-to-infection, adjusting for confounders. Key exposures will include prior antibiotic use (modeled via days of therapy), specific device types, insertion and maintenance protocols, and patient-level factors like neutropenia history, drawing on methods from prior studies of carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and gram-negative bacteria.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the paradigm from reactive infection prevention to proactive line preservation by providing the first comprehensive model of factors associated with successful long-term catheter use. It will generate actionable evidence to refine clinical guidelines for device selection and maintenance, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and infection control programs in high-risk settings.
proposals.novelty: This study inverts the traditional analytical framework for CLABSI research, focusing on protective factors for success rather than risk factors for failure, a perspective largely absent from the pre-2026 literature. It uniquely integrates data on prior antibiotic exposure, specific device characteristics, and detailed care processes into a single model of line preservation.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Burden of Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa: A Comparative Analysis of Attributable Mortality, Clinical Outcomes, and Healthcare Costs in a High-Risk Cohort
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a global crisis, but the specific drivers of its burden—whether primarily clinical (e.g., mortality, treatment failure) or economic—remain inadequately quantified for key pathogens like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA). Prior studies often conflate these burdens, complicating the prioritization of infection control and stewardship interventions, especially in vulnerable populations such as oncology and transplant patients where CRPA is endemic.
