proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that among patients with P. aeruginosa infections, the primary burden of carbapenem resistance is driven by increased attributable mortality and clinical complications, rather than by direct healthcare costs, after adjusting for underlying patient severity and prior healthcare exposures.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with P. aeruginosa bloodstream or pneumonia infections (2018-2024) at a tertiary care center. The primary exposure is CRPA vs. carbapenem-susceptible P. aeruginosa (CSPA). Primary outcomes will be 30-day all-cause mortality (clinical burden) and total hospitalization costs from admission (economic burden). We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders (e.g., prior antibiotic use, neutropenia, ICU admission). A comparative attribution analysis will quantify the proportion of burden explained by clinical vs. economic outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a direct, quantitative comparison of the clinical and economic burdens of CRPA, informing more targeted resource allocation for infection prevention. It will extend Dr. Kaye's prior work on resistant gram-negative bacteria in nursing homes and stewardship in oncology by focusing on a high-mortality pathogen in a high-acuity setting.
proposals.novelty: While prior cost-of-illness studies exist, this analysis directly contrasts and attributes burden components within the same cohort using robust causal inference methods, moving beyond descriptive associations to identify the primary driver of the AMR problem for CRPA.

proposals.title: Development and Validation of Setting-Specific Empiric Antibiotic Algorithms to Reduce Inappropriate Initial Therapy and Mortality in High-Risk Populations
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate empiric antibiotic therapy, driven by rising antimicrobial resistance, is a key modifiable risk factor for mortality in severe infections. Current guidelines are often one-size-fits-all, failing to account for the vastly different local resistance patterns and patient risk profiles across settings like nursing homes, oncology wards, and general hospitals. This leads to critical delays in effective treatment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that implementing data-driven, setting-specific empiric antibiotic algorithms, which integrate local resistance epidemiology and individual patient risk factors for resistant pathogens, will significantly reduce the rate of inappropriate initial therapy and associated 30-day all-cause mortality compared to standard guideline care.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, stepped-wedge cluster randomized trial. First, we will use retrospective electronic health record data (including prior antibiotic exposure, recent culture results, and comorbidities) from each participating setting (nursing homes, hematology-oncology, general medicine) to develop and validate logistic regression models predicting infection with carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA), extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL) Escherichia coli, and other key resistant organisms. These models will form the core of setting-specific treatment algorithms. Clusters (hospital wards or nursing homes) will be randomized to sequentially cross over from standard care to the intervention, where the algorithm provides clinician decision support for empiric therapy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable, data-driven methodology for creating local empiric guidelines and deliver validated, setting-specific algorithms ready for implementation. It will generate high-level evidence on whether such tailored decision support can concretely improve clinical outcomes, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship programs.
proposals.novelty: While risk scores for resistant infections exist, this study uniquely integrates them into pragmatic, setting-tailored treatment algorithms and tests their efficacy in a robust, prospective trial design across diverse care environments, moving beyond single-center, single-pathogen models.

proposals.title: A Patient-Specific Machine Learning Model to Predict Risk of Mixed Gram-Negative and Viral Respiratory Infections in High-Risk Oncology Patients
proposals.motivation: Mixed infections, particularly involving resistant Gram-negative bacteria (e.g., CRPA, CR-E. coli) and respiratory viruses (e.g., influenza), are associated with significantly higher mortality in immunocompromised patients. Current risk models are often pathogen-specific or population-level, failing to capture the complex, individual interplay of host factors, prior antibiotic exposure, and environmental exposures that predispose a specific patient to a polymicrobial infection. This gap hinders targeted, pre-emptive stewardship and infection control interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a machine learning model integrating detailed patient-level clinical, microbiological, and treatment history data can accurately predict an individual's short-term risk for developing a mixed Gram-negative and viral respiratory infection.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult hematopoietic stem cell transplant and oncology patients from 2020-2025. The outcome is a laboratory-confirmed mixed infection (Gram-negative bacteremia plus a positive respiratory viral PCR) within 30 days of admission. Predictors will include prior antibiotic/antiviral use, colonization history with multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs), recent influenza vaccination status, neutropenia duration, room type (single vs. multi-patient), and prior infection history. We will train and compare several models, including a regularized logistic regression (LASSO) as a baseline and a gradient boosting machine (XGBoost) to capture non-linear interactions, using temporal cross-validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a clinically actionable, individualized risk score that identifies patients at highest risk for complex mixed infections. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs by highlighting patients who may benefit from enhanced diagnostic testing or tailored empiric therapy. Furthermore, it will elucidate patient-specific risk factor interactions that are masked in aggregate analyses.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has modeled risk for single pathogens or MDRO colonization, this proposal uniquely focuses on predicting the co-occurrence of bacterial and viral pathogens at the individual level. It synthesizes Dr. Kaye's expertise in environmental exposures (e.g., room type) and stewardship in oncology with advanced, interpretable machine learning methods for personalized prediction.

proposals.title: The Protective Effect of Prior Skin and Soft Tissue Infection Against Subsequent Respiratory Viral Illness in High-Risk Inpatient Populations: A Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: The 'hygiene hypothesis' and microbiome research suggest microbial exposure can modulate systemic immunity. In high-risk inpatient settings like nursing homes and oncology units, patients face dual threats from multi-drug resistant skin pathogens (e.g., CRPA) and respiratory viruses (e.g., influenza). It is unknown if a recent localized skin infection, or its antibiotic treatment, inadvertently alters host susceptibility to subsequent viral respiratory infections, potentially impacting overall mortality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a documented skin and soft tissue infection (SSTI) in the prior 90 days is associated with a reduced risk of laboratory-confirmed influenza and other viral respiratory infections in high-risk adult inpatients, and that this association may be modified by the antibiotic regimen used for the SSTI.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and microbiology data from 2018-2024. The cohort will include adult patients with prior hospitalization in nursing home or oncology/transplant units. Exposure is a prior SSTI (culture-confirmed). The primary outcome is laboratory-confirmed influenza or other respiratory viral infection. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards regression, adjusting for age, comorbidities, immunosuppression, and prior antibiotic exposure. A secondary analysis will use propensity score matching to compare patients with SSTIs caused by resistant vs. susceptible organisms and those treated with different antibiotic classes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first epidemiological evidence on whether a prior bacterial SSTI influences risk for viral respiratory disease in vulnerable populations. It could identify a novel, modifiable factor in infection risk stratification and generate hypotheses about microbiome-immune crosstalk. The findings may inform more nuanced antimicrobial stewardship and infection prevention strategies that consider sequential infection risks.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the typical paradigm of infection increasing susceptibility, instead testing infection as a potential protective factor. It uniquely bridges the fields of antimicrobial resistance (CRPA, SSTIs) and viral epidemiology (influenza) in a high-risk clinical context informed by Dr. Kaye's prior work on resistant bacteria in nursing homes and stewardship in oncology.

proposals.title: A Diagnostic Stewardship Intervention to Minimize Unnecessary Antibiotic Initiation in High-Risk Oncology Patients: A Quasi-Experimental Study
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship (AMS) in high-risk oncology and transplant patients has traditionally focused on de-escalating or optimizing therapy after initiation, often accepting broad-spectrum empiric use as necessary. This approach fails to address the primary driver of antibiotic exposure and resistance pressure: the initial decision to treat. Pre-2026, rising rates of carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and other multidrug-resistant organisms in these vulnerable populations, linked to prior antibiotic exposure and increased mortality, demand a paradigm shift.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a diagnostic stewardship bundle focused on improving the specificity of infection diagnosis before antibiotic ordering will significantly reduce the rate of unnecessary antibiotic initiations in febrile high-risk oncology patients without increasing adverse clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will conduct a quasi-experimental, interrupted time-series study on a hematopoietic stem cell transplant/oncology unit. The intervention is a multi-component diagnostic stewardship protocol activated for new fever evaluations, including: 1) mandatory pre-prescription review of clinical criteria (e.g., systemic inflammatory response syndrome criteria) and biomarker results (e.g., procalcitonin), 2) rapid molecular diagnostic testing on respiratory samples for viral pathogens (e.g., influenza, RSV) in patients with respiratory symptoms, and 3) structured communication templates for the consulting infectious diseases service. The primary outcome is the rate of antibiotic initiations per 1000 patient-days. Secondary outcomes include days of therapy, time to appropriate therapy, and 30-day mortality. Analysis will use segmented regression of the time-series data.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide evidence for a proactive, diagnostic-centric AMS model that targets antibiotic initiation rather than modification. It will generate a practical framework for integrating rapid diagnostics and clinical decision rules at the point of care for high-risk patients. The findings could directly inform stewardship guidelines in immunocompromised hosts, a population often excluded from restrictive interventions.
proposals.novelty: While diagnostic stewardship is recognized, its application as the *primary* lever to minimize antibiotic use, rather than merely to guide drug choice, is novel in the high-risk oncology setting. This moves beyond prior work on environmental wipes or epidemiology by directly intervening on the clinician's diagnostic threshold before an antibiotic order is ever written.

proposals.title: A Basket Trial Design for Meropenem-Vaborbactam: Evaluating Efficacy in High-Risk Subgroups Defined by Carbapenem-Resistance Mechanisms and Host Immunosuppression
proposals.motivation: Mortality from carbapenem-resistant Gram-negative infections remains unacceptably high, particularly among immunocompromised and critically ill patients. Traditional antibiotic trials, which enroll broad populations based on infection site, often fail to generate robust efficacy data for these high-risk subgroups where novel agents like meropenem-vaborbactam are most needed. This limits evidence-based treatment guidance for the patients at greatest risk of death.
proposals.hypothesis: Meropenem-vaborbactam will demonstrate superior clinical efficacy compared to best available therapy in a biologically defined 'basket' of high-risk patients, specifically those with infections due to Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase (KPC)-producing Enterobacterales and profound immunosuppression (e.g., hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients).
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, randomized controlled trial using a master protocol with a basket design. The primary cohort ('basket') will enroll patients with documented KPC-producing Enterobacterales infections and profound immunosuppression (e.g., neutropenia, transplant). A secondary, exploratory basket will enroll patients with high severity-of-illness scores (e.g., APACHE II >15). The primary endpoint will be 30-day all-cause mortality. Bayesian adaptive methods will be used for interim analyses to allow for sample size re-estimation within pre-defined subgroups based on early efficacy signals.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-grade evidence for meropenem-vaborbactam's efficacy in a precisely defined, highest-risk population, moving beyond site-of-infection labeling. It will establish a methodological framework for future antibiotic trials to efficiently evaluate therapies in biologically relevant patient subgroups, analogous to precision oncology. The results will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship and treatment algorithms for the most vulnerable patients.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the precision oncology basket trial paradigm—testing a therapy across diseases sharing a biomarker—to infectious diseases, using a resistance mechanism and host immune status as the defining biomarkers instead of infection site. This represents a fundamental shift from the traditional, organ-based regulatory trial design for antibiotics.

proposals.title: Validating Clinician Intuition for Meropenem-Vaborbactam Use: A Longitudinal Cohort Study of Infection Recurrence and Resistance Emergence
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly KPC-producing strains, pose a critical threat with high mortality. Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a key therapy, but guidelines for its use in ambiguous cases are limited. Clinicians often rely on intuition to initiate MEV, potentially identifying a high-risk phenotype not captured by standard criteria. Quantifying the validity of this intuition could uncover novel predictors of infection severity and resistance risk.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients initiated on MEV based on clinician intuition, despite ambiguous guideline criteria, represent a distinct phenotype with a higher probability of true infection, leading to lower long-term recurrence rates but a distinct trajectory of subsequent resistance emergence compared to patients treated strictly per guidelines.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of patients treated with MEV. Patients will be stratified into two groups: 'Intuitive' (MEV initiated outside strict guideline criteria) and 'Guideline' (MEV per explicit criteria). Using pre-2026 methods, we will perform propensity score matching to balance baseline characteristics. Primary outcomes are 90-day infection recurrence and 12-month emergence of resistance (defined by MIC changes or new carbapenem-resistant isolates). Analysis will use Cox proportional hazards models for time-to-event outcomes and competing risks regression for mortality-adjusted resistance emergence.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal, objective assessment of clinician intuition as a predictor of infection outcomes and resistance risk. It will identify clinical or host factors subconsciously recognized by providers, informing more personalized antibiotic stewardship. The findings could lead to refined treatment algorithms that incorporate intuitive triggers for earlier, targeted therapy in high-risk patients.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond evaluating intuition as a diagnostic tool to treating it as a measurable risk-stratification variable for long-term microbiological and clinical outcomes. It synergizes behavioral science (clinical decision-making) with longitudinal infectious diseases epidemiology to model resistance trajectories.

proposals.title: Long-term Clinical Outcomes and Resistance Emergence in High-Risk Patients Following Treatment with Meropenem-Vaborbactam: A Multi-Center, Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a critical novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor combination for treating carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly KPC-producing strains. While pivotal trials demonstrated efficacy, real-world data on long-term survival, infection recurrence, and the risk of subsequent resistance development are limited, especially in vulnerable populations like oncology and transplant patients. Understanding these longer-term outcomes is essential for antimicrobial stewardship and for defining the optimal role of this agent in the face of rising resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: Patients successfully treated for CRE infections with MEV will have lower long-term (e.g., 90-day, 1-year) mortality and recurrence rates compared to those treated with alternative regimens, but a subset will develop subsequent infections with organisms exhibiting elevated MEV MICs or novel resistance mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients with CRE infections treated with MEV. The primary exposure is MEV treatment, with a comparator cohort of patients treated with other active agents (e.g., ceftazidime-avibactam, polymyxin-based regimens). Primary outcomes are all-cause mortality at 90 days and 1 year, and microbiologic/clinical recurrence. Secondary outcomes include the emergence of resistance, defined as a subsequent infection with an isolate demonstrating a ≥4-fold increase in MEV MIC or a new resistance mechanism (e.g., metallo-beta-lactamase production). We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models to adjust for confounders including severity of illness, source of infection, and prior antibiotic exposure.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide crucial real-world evidence on the durability of MEV's clinical benefit and its associated resistance risks over a clinically relevant timeframe. It will inform stewardship programs on the long-term implications of MEV use and help identify patient subgroups at highest risk for poor outcomes or resistance emergence, guiding post-treatment monitoring strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies have assessed MEV's short-term efficacy, this work focuses on the understudied long-term horizon and systematically evaluates the risk of resistance emergence post-treatment, a key concern for novel beta-lactamase inhibitors that has not been comprehensively addressed in real-world cohorts.

proposals.title: Population-Level Impact and Cost-Effectiveness of Meropenem-Vaborbactam on Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Transmission in U.S. Acute Care Hospitals: A Dynamic Transmission and Decision-Analytic Model
proposals.motivation: Carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE), particularly Klebsiella pneumoniae carbapenemase (KPC)-producing strains, are a critical public health threat associated with high mortality. While meropenem-vaborbactam (MEV) is a highly effective treatment for KPC-CRE infections, its high cost and potential ecological impact necessitate a population-level assessment. A pre-2026 evidence gap exists regarding whether and how strategic use of this novel agent could reduce overall CRE transmission and mortality at a hospital-system level, and whether such benefits justify the cost.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a targeted, guideline-driven treatment strategy using meropenem-vaborbactam for documented KPC-CRE infections, compared to older, less effective regimens, will reduce hospital-level CRE prevalence and attributable mortality, and will be cost-effective from a U.S. healthcare payer perspective over a 5-year time horizon.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid, dynamic transmission model (compartmental SEIR-type) coupled with a decision-analytic cost-effectiveness model. The transmission component will simulate patient flow between hospital wards (e.g., ICU, general), incorporating parameters for CRE importation, transmission via healthcare workers/environment (informed by Dr. Kaye's prior work on wipes), and patient-level risk factors. The model will compare two scenarios: a 'Standard of Care' arm (using pre-MEV therapies like polymyxins, tigecycline) and an 'Intervention' arm (using MEV for eligible KPC-CRE infections). We will use Monte Carlo microsimulation and probabilistic sensitivity analysis to estimate incremental cost-effectiveness ratios (ICERs) and uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, population-level estimate of the public health impact and economic value of a novel beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor for CRE. It will offer hospital administrators and antimicrobial stewardship programs a quantitative framework to guide formulary decisions and infection control resource allocation. The findings will directly inform national guidelines on the optimal use of newer, expensive antimicrobials against multidrug-resistant organisms.
proposals.novelty: This work moves beyond individual patient outcomes to model the herd effect of an effective antibiotic on resistant pathogen transmission within a hospital, integrating Dr. Kaye's expertise in transmission dynamics and infection prevention with health economic evaluation specific to a high-risk, high-cost therapeutic.

proposals.title: A Randomized, Non-Inferiority Trial of 7-Day versus 14-Day Antibiotic Therapy for Carbapenem-Resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) Bacteremia in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: CRE infections are associated with high mortality and limited treatment options, leading to prolonged antibiotic courses that drive further resistance and adverse events. Prior studies in other gram-negative infections suggest shorter durations may be non-inferior, but evidence is lacking specifically for CRE, particularly in vulnerable populations like oncology and transplant patients where infection and mortality risks are compounded.
proposals.hypothesis: A 7-day course of active antibiotic therapy for CRE bacteremia is non-inferior to a 14-day course in terms of a composite endpoint of all-cause mortality and clinical relapse at 30 days post-treatment completion.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, randomized, open-label, non-inferiority trial. Adult patients with CRE bacteremia (confirmed by culture and phenotypic/genotypic resistance testing) will be randomized 1:1 to 7 or 14 days of active therapy after source control. The primary outcome is a composite of all-cause mortality and clinical relapse at day 30 post-treatment. Using a non-inferiority margin of 10%, a power of 80%, and a one-sided alpha of 0.025, we will require approximately 350 patients. Secondary outcomes include microbiologic relapse, acquisition of new resistant organisms, and antibiotic-associated adverse events.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-level evidence to guide duration of therapy for CRE bacteremia, a critical gap in antimicrobial stewardship. It has the potential to establish a safer, shorter standard of care that reduces antibiotic exposure, associated toxicity, and selective pressure for resistance in this high-risk population.
proposals.novelty: While shorter-course therapy has been explored for susceptible gram-negative bacteremia, this trial specifically targets the high-stakes scenario of CRE infections in a vulnerable patient cohort, directly addressing a key challenge in stewardship identified in Dr. Kaye's prior work.

proposals.title: Long-Term Infectious Complications of Asymptomatic Bacteriuria: A Multi-Center Observational Cohort Study to Assess the Risk of Rare, Severe Sequelae
proposals.motivation: Current guidelines strongly recommend against treating asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB) in most patient populations, based on evidence from randomized controlled trials (RCTs) showing no short-term benefit. However, these RCTs are typically limited to 30-day follow-up and are underpowered to detect rare, severe infectious complications that may manifest months or years later, such as endocarditis, osteomyelitis, or metastatic abscesses. This knowledge gap is critical, as inappropriate treatment of ASB drives antimicrobial resistance, while potential long-term harms of non-treatment remain unquantified, particularly in high-risk populations like the elderly, transplant recipients, and those with prior resistant infections.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that untreated asymptomatic bacteriuria is associated with an increased long-term risk of rare, severe infectious complications compared to treated ASB, after controlling for key confounders including prior resistant infection history, comorbidities, and healthcare exposure.
proposals.method: We propose a multi-center, retrospective and prospective observational cohort study following adults with documented ASB (≥10^5 CFU/mL without symptoms) for 5-10 years. Data will be extracted from electronic health records across 15-20 hospitals, capturing ASB episodes, antibiotic treatment, and subsequent infections. The primary outcome is a composite of rare, severe complications (e.g., endocarditis, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, epidural abscess). We will use time-to-event analysis (Cox proportional hazards models) with propensity score matching (using algorithms like greedy nearest-neighbor matching) to control for confounding by indication, adjusting for variables such as age, Charlson Comorbidity Index, prior CRPA or ESBL-E. coli colonization, nursing home residence, and immunosuppression.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, long-term epidemiological evidence on the risk of severe sequelae from ASB, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will quantify a potential long-term trade-off between antibiotic stewardship and patient safety. The findings will be crucial for developing risk-stratification tools to identify patient subgroups where ASB treatment might be warranted to prevent downstream mortality and morbidity.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel by applying a cardiology-inspired, long-term observational framework to a urological/infectious disease question, moving beyond the short-term horizon of existing RCTs. It specifically leverages real-world data and advanced causal inference methods to investigate rare outcomes that are impractical to study in conventional trials, challenging a core tenet of antimicrobial stewardship.

proposals.title: Decomposing Clinical Intuition: A Causal Inference Study to Validate Gestalt in the Diagnosis of Suspected Infection Among High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship guidelines often conflict with clinician gestalt, particularly in complex patients with prior resistant infections or high mortality risk, such as those in oncology or nursing homes. This tension drives guideline-discordant empiric therapy, a major contributor to antimicrobial resistance. Quantifying the valid predictive signal within clinical intuition, separate from cognitive biases, is a critical unmet need to improve both stewardship and patient outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific, prospectively documented components of clinical gestalt (e.g., narrative cues, pattern recognition) provide significant independent predictive value for adjudicated true infection status, after rigorously controlling for guideline-recommended variables and known confounders.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective cohort study of patients with suspected infection in high-risk settings (oncology, transplant, nursing homes). At the decision point, treating clinicians will complete a structured form documenting observations, patient narrative, and reasoning justifying guideline-discordant treatment. A blinded multidisciplinary panel will adjudicate true infection using pre-specified criteria (e.g., CDC definitions) and longitudinal data. Using causal inference methods—specifically targeted maximum likelihood estimation (TMLE) and causal forest algorithms—we will estimate the average treatment effect of 'high gestalt' on adjudicated infection, adjusting for guideline variables (vital signs, labs, imaging) and confounders (prior CRPA/E. coli, influenza status, functional status).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first causal, quantitative framework for validating clinical gestalt in infection diagnosis, moving beyond anecdote. It will identify which intuitive components are most predictive, offering a evidence-based rationale for when to deviate from guidelines. The findings will directly inform stewardship programs and diagnostic decision support tools.
proposals.novelty: Prior studies on clinical intuition are largely retrospective and correlational. This study prospectively captures the *components* of gestalt at the decision point and uses modern causal inference methods to isolate its predictive value from confounding biases, a novel application in infectious diseases diagnostics.

proposals.title: Decoding Clinical Intuition: A Machine Learning Approach to Validate Unmeasured Predictors of True Infection in Patients with Suspected Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA)
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial stewardship programs face a critical challenge when clinicians deviate from guidelines to treat patients with suspected resistant infections, such as CRPA. These decisions may reflect unmeasured clinical predictors of true infection and mortality risk not captured by standard criteria. Validating this 'clinical intuition' is essential to improve stewardship, reduce unnecessary antibiotic use, and better target therapy for high-risk patients, particularly in vulnerable populations like oncology and transplant patients where prior antibiotic exposure is common.
proposals.hypothesis: Clinician decisions to initiate or continue anti-CRPA therapy, despite guideline recommendations for de-escalation or cessation, correlate with a latent clinical phenotype characterized by specific, measurable host-response and microbiological factors that predict true infection and 30-day all-cause mortality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study of adult inpatients with positive CRPA cultures from 2018-2025, using electronic health record data. The primary exposure is clinician treatment decision (treat vs. not treat) after culture results, adjusted for guideline-based factors (e.g., fever, leukocytosis). We will train a gradient boosting machine (XGBoost) model using structured data (vitals, labs, prior antibiotics, comorbidities) and novel features derived from natural language processing (NLP) of clinician notes (e.g., descriptions of wound appearance, patient trajectory). The model will predict a composite outcome of true infection (per validated criteria) or 30-day mortality. We will use SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) to identify the top features driving treatment decisions and their association with the outcome.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence for the validity of clinician intuition in infection management, moving beyond a purely guideline-based stewardship paradigm. It will identify specific, actionable clinical features that could be incorporated into future decision-support tools to improve early identification of patients with true, high-mortality CRPA infections.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has assessed guideline adherence, this study uniquely applies interpretable machine learning to 'reverse-engineer' the clinical reasoning behind guideline deviations, specifically for CRPA. It integrates NLP-derived unstructured data with traditional risk factors to define a latent phenotype of true infection.

proposals.title: A Multi-Layer Clinical Decision Support System, Modeled on Intrusion Detection, to Reduce Inappropriate Antibiotic Treatment for Asymptomatic Bacteriuria: A Pragmatic Trial
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate antibiotic treatment for asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB) is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance, adverse drug events, and Clostridioides difficile infection, particularly in high-risk populations like nursing home residents and oncology patients. Current antimicrobial stewardship interventions are often reactive or lack real-time, patient-specific integration into clinical workflows, leading to persistent overprescribing. A novel, automated, and proactive approach is urgently needed to intercept inappropriate orders at the point of care.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a clinical decision support system (CDSS) combining a rule-based signature detector and an anomaly-based machine learning model, adapted from cybersecurity intrusion detection principles, will significantly reduce the rate of inappropriate antibiotic orders for ASB without negatively impacting patient safety or mortality.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-layer CDSS integrated into the Epic EHR. The rule-based layer will use logic (e.g., absence of UTI symptoms, specific urinalysis findings) to flag classic ASB scenarios. The anomaly detection layer will employ an isolation forest or one-class SVM algorithm trained on historical, appropriate prescribing data to identify statistical outliers in ordering patterns. In a stepped-wedge, cluster-randomized trial across hospital units, we will measure the system's impact on antibiotic days of therapy for ASB, with secondary outcomes including rates of subsequent resistant gram-negative infections (e.g., CRPA, CRE) and 30-day mortality.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, scalable tool for real-time ASB stewardship, directly addressing a critical gap in infection prevention. It will generate new evidence on the feasibility and effectiveness of adapting cybersecurity paradigms for clinical decision-making. The findings will advance the field by demonstrating how hybrid rule-based/anomaly detection systems can be operationalized to modify complex clinician behavior and improve antibiotic use.
proposals.novelty: The conceptual novelty lies in the direct translation of cybersecurity intrusion detection system architecture—specifically the hybrid signature/anomaly paradigm—to a clinical stewardship problem. Technically, applying unsupervised anomaly detection algorithms to identify aberrant prescribing patterns for ASB in real-time represents a significant departure from existing, simpler rule-based CDSS alerts.

proposals.title: A Randomized Trial of a Default Rule Nudge in the Electronic Health Record to Reduce Inappropriate Antibiotic Treatment for Asymptomatic Bacteriuria
proposals.motivation: Inappropriate antibiotic treatment for asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB) is a major driver of antimicrobial resistance, adverse drug events, and Clostridioides difficile infection, particularly in high-risk populations like nursing home residents and oncology patients. Despite clear guidelines against treatment, clinician inertia and diagnostic uncertainty lead to persistent overprescribing, contributing to patient mortality from resistant infections. Behavioral economics offers proven, low-cost interventions to shift decision-making, but their application to antimicrobial stewardship (ASB) remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a default rule nudge in the electronic health record (EHR)—where the order set defaults to 'no antibiotic treatment' for patients meeting ASB criteria—will significantly reduce the rate of inappropriate antibiotic initiation compared to a standard, non-default order interface.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, cluster-randomized crossover trial in a large healthcare system. Using EHR data (vital signs, urinalysis, microbiology, problem lists), a real-time clinical decision support (CDS) algorithm will identify inpatient and nursing home patients meeting pre-specified ASB criteria (e.g., absence of systemic signs). In intervention periods, the antibiotic order panel will default to 'No treatment recommended for ASB,' requiring an active opt-out and mandatory free-text justification to prescribe. Control periods will use the standard panel. The primary outcome is the proportion of ASB episodes resulting in antibiotic orders. Analysis will use generalized linear mixed models adjusting for clustering.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-quality evidence on the efficacy of a default rule nudge for ASB management, directly testing a behavioral intervention to de-implement low-value care. It will generate a replicable, scalable EHR-based CDS architecture that leverages clinician inertia for stewardship. Findings will inform policy on using choice architecture to reduce antibiotic resistance and patient harm.
proposals.novelty: While default nudges are established in environmental policy, their application to antimicrobial prescribing is novel. This approach moves beyond traditional ASB alerts (which cause alert fatigue) by reframing the choice architecture to make guideline-concordant care the path of least resistance, a strategy not yet evaluated in pre-2026 stewardship trials.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Harm of Withheld Treatment: A Retrospective Counterfactual Analysis of Severe Outcomes Following Negative Urine Cultures in High-Risk Patients
proposals.motivation: In patients with suspected urinary tract infections (UTIs), a negative urine culture typically leads to the withholding of antibiotics. However, in high-risk populations (e.g., nursing home residents, oncology patients), this decision may be harmful if the culture is a false negative or if a subsequent infection develops rapidly. Pre-2026, the risk of severe outcomes (hospitalization, sepsis, death) attributable to this non-intervention is poorly quantified, hindering the refinement of diagnostic and treatment thresholds.
proposals.hypothesis: Among high-risk patients with an initial negative urine culture who later develop a confirmed UTI, a significant proportion of severe subsequent outcomes are attributable to the decision to withhold empirical antibiotic treatment at the time of the initial negative result.
proposals.method: We will conduct a retrospective cohort study using electronic health records from a large healthcare system, focusing on patients with hematologic malignancies, solid organ transplants, or residing in nursing homes. For each index patient with a negative culture followed by a confirmed UTI within 30 days, we will construct a synthetic control using propensity score matching (pre-2026 methods: logistic regression for propensity scores, nearest-neighbor matching) on demographics, comorbidities, prior antibiotic exposure, and healthcare utilization. This synthetic control represents the counterfactual scenario of early empirical treatment. We will then compare the incidence of severe outcomes (composite of hospitalization for UTI, bacteremia, or 30-day all-cause mortality) between the observed (no treatment) and synthetic (hypothetical treatment) groups using weighted Cox proportional hazards models.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first quantitative estimate of the attributable risk of severe outcomes due to withholding antibiotics after a negative urine culture in high-risk populations. It will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship programs by identifying patient subgroups for whom current diagnostic thresholds may be unsafe, potentially leading to more nuanced, risk-stratified treatment guidelines.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its application of climate science-inspired counterfactual attribution modeling to a clinical microbiology and stewardship question, specifically inverting the typical framework to study harm from non-action rather than benefit from action.

proposals.title: Determinants of the Diagnostic-Therapeutic Gap in Symptomatic Urinary Tract Infections: A Mixed-Methods Study to Inform Stewardship Interventions
proposals.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a preeminent global health threat, driven in part by inappropriate antibiotic use. While stewardship often focuses on over-treatment, under-treatment of symptomatic infections also poses significant risks, including treatment failure, progression to severe disease, and increased mortality, particularly in vulnerable populations. This study addresses the critical but understudied 'diagnostic-therapeutic gap'—the failure to provide adequate, timely antibiotic therapy for patients with clear symptoms—which may be a key driver of poor outcomes and a hidden contributor to AMR through sequential, ineffective treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant, measurable gap exists between the presentation of symptomatic UTI and the delivery of guideline-concordant, adequate antibiotic therapy, and that this gap is primarily driven by identifiable and modifiable cognitive, logistical, and systemic determinants within clinical workflows.
proposals.method: Phase 1 (Quantitative): A retrospective cohort study will audit electronic health records of adult patients with symptomatic, uncomplicated UTI diagnoses in outpatient and nursing home settings (pre-2026). Using a Delphi-derived operational definition of 'adequate treatment' (encompassing timely diagnosis, appropriate first-line agent, correct dose/duration), we will calculate the prevalence of under-treatment. Multivariable logistic regression will identify patient-level (e.g., prior resistant infections, comorbidities) and facility-level risk factors. Phase 2 (Qualitative): Guided by the quantitative findings, we will conduct semi-structured interviews with clinicians and process mapping exercises to elucidate the root causes of identified gaps, using thematic analysis to categorize determinants.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first operationalized metric and benchmark for 'adequate treatment' of symptomatic UTI, moving beyond simple appropriateness to assess care quality. It will generate a determinant-focused framework for designing targeted stewardship interventions that address under-treatment, thereby potentially improving patient outcomes and making antibiotic use more precise and effective.
proposals.novelty: This work is novel in its specific focus on under-treatment as a stewardship target and its application of a determinant-focused, mixed-methods approach to move from describing a problem to identifying actionable system-level intervention points, bridging the gap between epidemiology and implementation science.

proposals.title: Personalizing Infection Prevention: A Risk-Stratified, Microbiome-Informed Intervention to Reduce Resistant Gram-Negative Infections in High-Risk Oncology Patients
proposals.motivation: Standardized infection prevention bundles are widely implemented but assume uniform patient risk, potentially missing opportunities for targeted protection. Pre-2026 evidence shows oncology and transplant patients have high mortality from resistant gram-negative infections like carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and Escherichia coli, and that prior colonization and microbiome disruption are key risk factors. A one-size-fits-all approach may be inefficient and insufficient for these vulnerable populations.
proposals.hypothesis: A prevention strategy personalized to an individual's gut microbiome composition and prior colonization history will be more effective at preventing subsequent resistant gram-negative infections and associated mortality than a standardized bundle alone in high-risk oncology patients.
proposals.method: We will conduct a prospective, matched cohort study in a hematology-oncology unit. Patients will be screened for rectal colonization with multidrug-resistant organisms (MDROs) and have baseline stool metagenomic sequencing. Using pre-2026 machine learning methods (e.g., Random Forest or LASSO regression), we will develop a risk score integrating microbiome features (e.g., diversity, resistance gene abundance), prior MDRO status, and clinical factors (e.g., recent fluoroquinolone use). High-risk patients will receive a personalized bundle (e.g., targeted decolonization, enhanced environmental cleaning, probiotic intervention) while others receive the standard bundle. The primary outcome will be the incidence of clinical infection with resistant gram-negative bacteria within 90 days.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical evidence for a microbiome-informed, risk-stratified infection prevention protocol. It will move the field beyond generic bundles towards precision stewardship. The resulting risk model could be deployed to efficiently allocate intensive prevention resources to patients who would benefit most.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye et al. identified risks in specific settings (nursing homes, multi-patient rooms), this proposal actively uses the gut microbiome as a modifiable risk factor for personalizing prevention, a concept not yet translated into interventional trials in oncology by 2025.

proposals.title: Personalized Prophylactic Antibiotic Dosing for Surgical Site Infection Prevention: A Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling Study in High-Risk Surgical Cohorts
proposals.motivation: Surgical site infections (SSIs) remain a major cause of morbidity, mortality, and healthcare costs, particularly in high-risk populations like oncology and transplant patients. Current prophylactic antibiotic guidelines are often one-size-fits-all, potentially leading to suboptimal tissue concentrations and contributing to both SSI risk and the selection of resistant pathogens, including carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) and extended-spectrum beta-lactamase (ESBL)-producing Enterobacteriaceae. Optimizing dosing, timing, and duration is critical to improve outcomes and combat antimicrobial resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a patient- and procedure-specific pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) model, incorporating individual patient factors (e.g., renal function, body composition, prior antibiotic exposure) and surgical characteristics, can identify optimized prophylactic antibiotic regimens that achieve target tissue exposure more reliably than standard dosing, thereby reducing SSI rates.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, retrospective cohort study of adult patients undergoing major abdominal or orthopedic surgery. Using electronic health record data, we will build a population PK model for commonly used prophylactic agents (e.g., cefazolin, piperacillin-tazobactam). We will integrate covariates like creatinine clearance, weight, and procedure duration. A Monte Carlo simulation will then be performed to calculate the probability of target attainment (PTA) for various dosing regimens against relevant pathogens, including E. coli and resistant gram-negatives. The primary outcome will be the association between model-predicted PTA and observed SSI rates, analyzed using multivariable logistic regression.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a data-driven framework for personalizing surgical antibiotic prophylaxis, moving beyond generic guidelines. It will generate specific, evidence-based dosing recommendations for high-risk sub-populations, such as those with obesity or renal impairment. The findings will directly inform antimicrobial stewardship efforts in surgical and oncology settings by balancing efficacy with resistance prevention.
proposals.novelty: While PK/PD modeling is established for therapeutic antibiotics, its application to optimize *prophylactic* dosing for SSI prevention, particularly by integrating real-world patient data and resistance patterns from high-risk cohorts, is novel. This approach directly addresses the challenge of prior antibiotic exposure and resistant colonization highlighted in Dr. Kaye's prior work.

proposals.title: Optimizing Host Resilience and Wound Healing Trajectories to Mitigate Mortality in High-Risk Patients with Prior Multidrug-Resistant Infections
proposals.motivation: Infection prevention research, especially in high-risk populations like transplant or oncology patients, often focuses narrowly on preventing new resistant infections. However, patient outcomes are determined not just by infection incidence but by the host's ability to recover from existing insults. A dual approach that actively promotes healing and resilience could be critical for reducing mortality where prevention alone is insufficient.
proposals.hypothesis: In patients with a prior history of multidrug-resistant Gram-negative (e.g., CRPA, CR-E. coli) infection, a composite intervention targeting nutritional, metabolic, and local wound healing factors will significantly improve wound healing rates and reduce 90-day all-cause mortality compared to standard infection prevention protocols alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-center, prospective cohort study with an embedded, randomized controlled trial (RCT) component. High-risk patients (e.g., post-HSCT, major surgery) with prior resistant infections will be enrolled. All will receive enhanced infection prevention (e.g., dedicated wipes, stewardship). A randomized subset will additionally receive a bundled 'resilience' intervention: protocolized nutritional supplementation (high-protein, arginine), glycemic control targeting, and standardized advanced wound care (e.g., negative pressure therapy where indicated). The primary outcome is a composite of complete wound closure and 90-day survival. We will use multivariable Cox proportional hazards and logistic regression models, adjusting for prior pathogen, comorbidities, and neutropenia status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for shifting from a purely defensive to a proactive-recovery model in infection management. It will generate evidence for a practical, bundled intervention targeting host resilience and establish whether improving healing trajectories directly impacts mortality in this vulnerable population.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Kaye et al. has evaluated environmental prevention (wipes) and stewardship challenges, this proposal uniquely integrates these with a direct, concurrent intervention on the host's recovery capacity, treating enhanced healing as an active component of infection outcome mitigation rather than a passive result.

proposals.title: Comparative Effectiveness of Ceftolozane-Tazobactam Monotherapy versus Combination Therapy for Severe Carbapenem-Resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa Infections: A Multicenter Retrospective Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Ceftolozane-tazobactam (C/T) is a critical agent for treating carbapenem-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa (CRPA) infections, but treatment failures and resistance emergence are reported. In severe infections, clinicians often empirically add a second agent (e.g., an aminoglycoside, polymyxin, or fosfomycin) despite a lack of robust evidence supporting this practice, which may increase toxicity without clear benefit. Defining optimal C/T-based strategies is urgent to improve survival while preserving this last-line antibiotic.
proposals.hypothesis: For patients with severe CRPA infections (e.g., bacteremia, pneumonia), initial combination therapy with C/T and a second in vitro active agent will be associated with lower 30-day all-cause mortality and reduced emergence of C/T resistance compared to C/T monotherapy.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multicenter, retrospective cohort study of adult patients (2015-2025) with severe CRPA infection treated with C/T for ≥72 hours. Using data from electronic health records and microbiology, we will define cohorts as C/T monotherapy or combination therapy. The primary outcome is 30-day mortality. We will use multivariable logistic regression and propensity score matching to adjust for confounders (e.g., severity, source, prior antibiotics, neutropenia). Secondary outcomes include clinical failure, nephrotoxicity, and microbiologic recurrence with resistant isolates. Subgroup analyses will focus on hematology-oncology and transplant patients.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the largest comparative effectiveness data to date on C/T combination strategies, directly informing antimicrobial stewardship and treatment guidelines for CRPA. It will quantify the potential mortality benefit of combination therapy and characterize the associated risk of nephrotoxicity. The findings will help define high-risk patient subgroups who may benefit most from aggressive initial therapy.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies describe C/T use, few directly compare mono- versus combination therapy outcomes in a real-world, high-acuity population with rigorous adjustment for confounding by indication. This study leverages Dr. Kaye's expertise in resistant infections and vulnerable patient populations to address a pressing clinical dilemma where guideline evidence is lacking.

ideas.title: Using Long-Term Observational Studies to Detect Rare Complications from ASB
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from cardiology, where long-term observational studies were used to detect rare complications from subclinical conditions. In cardiology, the question of whether treating asymptomatic atherosclerosis prevents rare, severe events was addressed not through short-term RCTs but through large-scale, long-term observational cohort studies and registries that tracked patients for years. This approach is adapted to the ASB context by proposing a multi-center, long-term (5-10 year) observational cohort study of patients with ASB. The study would follow patients to capture rare, severe downstream infectious complications (e.g., endocarditis, osteomyelitis) that manifest well beyond 30 days. Using advanced statistical methods to control for confounders, the research would compare the incidence of these complications between patients who received antibiotic treatment for ASB and those who did not, thereby challenging the assumption that ASB treatment has no impact on severe long-term outcomes.

ideas.title: Leveraging Clinician Intuition to Predict Long-Term Outcomes and Resistance Risk in Meropenem-Vaborbactam Treated Patients
ideas.description: This study investigates whether clinician intuition to treat with meropenem-vaborbactam, even when guideline criteria are ambiguous, identifies a distinct patient phenotype with a higher likelihood of true infection. It then longitudinally tracks this cohort to determine if this intuitively identified subset experiences different long-term outcomes, specifically lower recurrence rates and a distinct trajectory of resistance emergence, compared to patients treated strictly by guidelines. The synergy lies in using long-term microbiological and survival data to objectively test and quantify the predictive validity of provider intuition, which could reveal unmeasured clinical predictors of infection severity and host response that are not captured in current diagnostic algorithms. This approach could lead to new, intuition-informed models for personalizing antibiotic therapy and anticipating resistance risk.

ideas.title: A Causal Framework for Decomposing and Validating Clinical Gestalt in Suspected Infection
ideas.description: This study prospectively collects structured data on the specific clinical observations, patient narratives, and heuristic reasoning clinicians cite when deciding to treat outside guidelines for suspected infection. A multidisciplinary panel, blinded to treatment decisions, rigorously adjudicates true infection status using pre-specified criteria and all available longitudinal data. Using causal inference methods, the analysis tests whether the documented components of clinical gestalt provide significant predictive value for adjudicated infection after controlling for all guideline-recommended variables and known confounders, thereby isolating and validating the signal within intuition from its biases.

ideas.title: Operationalizing and Intervening on the Diagnostic-Therapeutic Gap for Symptomatic UTIs: A Determinant-Focused Mixed-Methods Study
ideas.description: This study first establishes a consensus-based, operational definition of 'adequate treatment' for symptomatic, uncomplicated UTIs, including diagnostic criteria and first-line antibiotic selection. It then employs a sequential explanatory mixed-methods design: a quantitative phase audits clinical records against this standard to quantify the prevalence and patterns of under-treatment, followed by a qualitative phase using clinician interviews and process mapping to identify the specific cognitive, logistical, and policy determinants driving the gap. The final output is a tailored intervention framework targeting the most impactful and modifiable determinants identified, moving from mere description to actionable system redesign.

ideas.title: Applying Precision Oncology Trial Design to Antibiotic Subgroup Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the 'basket trial' methodology from precision oncology to antibiotic research. In oncology, basket trials test a single targeted therapy across diverse cancer types that share a specific biomarker, efficiently identifying efficacy in biologically defined subgroups. The adapted research idea is to design a meropenem-vaborbactam study that enrolls a 'basket' of high-risk patients defined by specific, measurable risk factors (e.g., high severity-of-illness scores, specific carbapenem-resistance mechanisms, or profound immunosuppression) rather than just by infection site or general category. This precision approach would generate robust, targeted efficacy data for the most critical and difficult-to-treat patient subgroups where the novel antibiotic's impact is hypothesized to be greatest.

ideas.title: Retrospective Counterfactual Analysis of Negative Diagnostic Results
ideas.description: This research adapts the counterfactual attribution methodology from climate science to investigate the inverse clinical scenario. In climate attribution, scientists model a world without a specific factor (e.g., greenhouse gases) to quantify its impact on an observed extreme event. Similarly, this study would identify patients with the 'extreme event' of a subsequent UTI following a negative urine culture. By creating a synthetic control model simulating early empirical antibiotic treatment, researchers can perform a retrospective counterfactual analysis. The goal is to quantify the increased risk of severe outcomes (like hospitalization or complicated infection) attributable to the decision to withhold treatment based on the initial negative culture, thereby identifying a subpopulation harmed by non-intervention and refining future treatment thresholds.

ideas.title: Apply Default Rule Nudges from Environmental Policy to Reduce ASB Treatment
ideas.description: This idea adapts a proven behavioral economics solution from environmental policy—the use of default rules to promote pro-social behavior—to the clinical problem of unnecessary antibiotic prescribing for asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB). In energy conservation, setting 'green energy' as the default choice dramatically increases participation by exploiting status quo bias. The adapted solution implements this in clinical decision support by making 'no antibiotic treatment' the pre-selected, default option in the electronic ordering interface when ASB criteria are met. To prescribe antibiotics, the clinician must actively opt-out of the default, providing a brief, structured justification. This choice architecture reframes non-treatment as the standard of care, uses inertia to support guideline adherence, and introduces a friction point for unnecessary prescribing.

ideas.title: Adapting Cybersecurity Intrusion Detection Systems for ASB-Specific Clinical Decision Support
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from cybersecurity—specifically, Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS)—to the clinical problem of reducing inappropriate antibiotic treatment for asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB). In cybersecurity, IDS use rule-based and anomaly-based algorithms to monitor network traffic, flag deviations from baselines or known threat signatures, and trigger automated responses. The adapted idea involves designing and testing a multi-layered clinical decision support tool embedded within the electronic health record. This tool would employ: 1) a rule-based layer that fires alerts for classic ASB scenarios (e.g., positive urine culture in an asymptomatic patient without specific indications), and 2) an anomaly detection layer that uses machine learning to identify prescribing patterns that statistically deviate from evidence-based institutional protocols. The integrated system would provide real-time, context-sensitive alerts to clinicians at the point of antibiotic ordering, with the goal of intercepting inappropriate treatment decisions. The research would involve developing the algorithms, integrating them into a clinical workflow, and testing their impact on antibiotic prescribing rates and clinical outcomes.

ideas.title: Targeted ASB Treatment Based on Host-Microbiome Interaction Signatures
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that ASB treatment is never beneficial by adapting a solution from oncology, where treatment moved from a one-size-fits-all approach to biomarker-driven, targeted therapy. The analogous solution involved identifying specific molecular signatures (e.g., HER2, EGFR) that predict therapeutic benefit within a broader disease category. Adapted to ASB, the research would aim to identify and validate specific host (e.g., immune response signatures, genetic susceptibility) and/or microbial (e.g., pathogen virulence factor expression) biomarkers that define high-risk subgroups where bacteriuria is not benign colonization but a precursor to adverse outcomes. The goal is to develop a precision medicine framework for ASB, where treatment is recommended only for those with a measurable 'high-risk phenotype,' fundamentally refining the current blanket non-treatment guideline.

ideas.title: Adaptive CRE Trials with Integrated Predictive Surrogate Endpoint Modeling
ideas.description: This redesigned approach embeds a dynamically updated, validated predictive model of the definitive clinical endpoint within a Bayesian adaptive trial platform for CRE treatments. The system continuously fuses early biomarker, microbiological, and clinical stability data to generate real-time predictions of individual patient outcomes, which are then used to drive robust adaptation decisions. This directly overcomes the fatal flaw of endpoint latency and noise by providing the adaptive algorithm with a timely, high-fidelity signal for decision-making. The result is an adaptive design that is both statistically efficient and operationally feasible for complex, slow-to-evaluate infections like CRE.

ideas.title: Modeling the ecological and evolutionary consequences of treating asymptomatic bacteriuria using principles from invasive species management
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from ecology and conservation biology, where models predict the long-term consequences of invasive species management. The analogous solution involves using population and evolutionary models to forecast secondary effects like ecosystem disruption and resistance evolution. Adapted to the original research seed, this proposes developing a computational model of the urinary tract as a microbial ecosystem to project the longer-term consequences of treating asymptomatic bacteriuria. The model would simulate different treatment strategies to quantify risks such as antimicrobial resistance development, collateral damage to commensal bacteria, and the subsequent risk of symptomatic infections, ultimately aiming to define treatment protocols that optimize individual and public health outcomes by considering ecological and evolutionary trade-offs.

ideas.title: Adapt Cognitive Work Analysis from aviation to map antibiotic prescribing decisions
ideas.description: This research adapts the Cognitive Work Analysis (CWA) framework from aviation human factors and cognitive systems engineering to understand prescribing decisions for asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB). The analogous solution involves systematically modeling a complex, safety-critical work domain (like a cockpit) to reveal how system constraints—physical, informational, social, and organizational—shape expert decisions. Adapted to this context, the research would employ CWA to map the multi-level constraints influencing ASB treatment, including guidelines, electronic health record interfaces, lab workflow, team communication, and time pressure, thereby identifying systemic leverage points for intervention that support appropriate prescribing, moving beyond individual education-based approaches.

ideas.title: National ASB Treatment Atlas: A Geospatial and Temporal Analysis Using Ecological Survey Methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts solutions from ecology and environmental science, specifically the methods used in national-scale, gridded ecological surveys like NEON. The analogous solution involves deploying standardized measurement protocols across geographically dispersed sites to collect comparable data, then applying spatial statistics and GIS to analyze patterns and correlations with regional environmental variables. Adapted to the medical research context, the idea is to treat a national hospital cohort database as a network of standardized sampling nodes. The research would implement a unified data protocol to extract ASB treatment metrics, then use geospatial mapping and hierarchical Bayesian modeling to create a National ASB Treatment Atlas. This atlas would visually and statistically identify geographic hotspots of inappropriate antibiotic use, analyze their correlation with regional healthcare system factors (e.g., stewardship program density, formulary restrictions), and control for local patient demographics, thereby confirming initial findings and rigorously assessing the drivers of geographic variation in ASB treatment practices.

ideas.title: Applying Signal Detection Theory to Reduce Missed Diagnoses of Symptomatic UTI
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from Signal Detection Theory in psychology, this research addresses the dual problem of symptomatic UTI under-treatment by framing it as a systematic increase in 'misses' due to an overly strict clinical decision criterion. The analogous solution—recalibrating the decision threshold and enhancing signal clarity—is adapted to the medical context. The study will examine the factors that shift the diagnostic criterion away from treatment (e.g., fear of antibiotic resistance, diagnostic uncertainty) and pilot an intervention combining a refined clinical algorithm with point-of-care diagnostic tools (like advanced urinalysis or novel biomarkers) to better distinguish true infections. The goal is to develop a balanced protocol that mitigates under-treatment without reverting to over-prescription, effectively optimizing the trade-off between these two quality gaps.

ideas.title: Adapt ecological succession and pest resistance models to forecast long-term antibiotic treatment outcomes
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solutions from Agricultural Ecology and Integrated Pest Management (IPM), where long-term outcomes of interventions are assessed by modeling pest population dynamics and resistance gene frequencies under selective pressure. The analogous solution involves monitoring ecological succession and genetic drift in treated fields to forecast resistance and guide management strategies. Adapted to the original idea, this means developing longitudinal, model-driven studies that track not just clinical endpoints but the evolving bacterial population ecology and resistome in patients following meropenem-vaborbactam therapy. The goal is to use these ecological and population genetics models to predict the conditions and timelines for resistance emergence and disease recurrence, informing strategies for antibiotic stewardship and combination therapies.

ideas.title: Adapting credit risk anomaly detection to predict inappropriate ASB treatment
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from financial fraud and credit risk assessment, where ensemble machine learning models are used to detect anomalous transactions deviating from legitimate patterns. The analogous solution involves training models like random forests on historical data to generate risk scores that flag high-probability events before they occur. Adapted to the clinical context, the idea is to apply similar ensemble ML techniques (e.g., XGBoost, Random Forest) to electronic health record data to model which patients are most likely to receive inappropriate ASB treatment. The model would be trained on historical EHR features—including patient demographics, comorbidities, prior urine culture results, antibiotic prescribing patterns, and clinical context—to identify complex, non-linear patterns predictive of inappropriate treatment. The output would be a clinical decision support tool that generates a risk score for inappropriate ASB treatment at the point of care, enabling prospective intervention through alerts or review pathways to reduce unnecessary antibiotic use.

ideas.title: A Cognitive Simulation Testbed for Predicting and Optimizing Antimicrobial Prescribing Decisions
ideas.description: This research integrates ethnographic human factors analysis with computational cognitive modeling to move beyond describing prescribing drivers to actively predicting and improving decisions. First, cognitive task analysis and workflow studies are used to build a formal, validated computational model of prescriber behavior. This agent-based model is then deployed as a simulation testbed, where proposed interventions—such as new clinical decision support tools or workflow modifications—can be tested virtually. The approach generates quantitative predictions about intervention impact on ASB treatment rates, enabling the data-driven design of the most robust and effective systems before piloting in live clinical environments.

ideas.title: Co-Designing Context-Aware Nudge Systems to De-implement ASB Treatment
ideas.description: This research integrates human factors engineering and behavioral economics to develop and test adaptive 'nudge systems' for reducing inappropriate antibiotic treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria (ASB). First, human factors methods (e.g., cognitive task analysis, systems mapping) are used to create a dynamic model of prescribing decisions, identifying not just barriers but real-time contextual triggers (e.g., workload, EHR alerts, team communication). These insights then guide the co-design of precisely targeted choice architecture interventions (e.g., order set defaults, feedback, commitment devices) that are embedded within the identified workflow and cognitive pinch-points. The synergy enables iterative testing and refinement, where the system's impact on behavior further informs the human factors model, creating a learning healthcare intervention tailored to the specific socio-technical ecosystem of each clinical setting.

ideas.title: Modeling the impact of undocumented symptoms on inappropriate ASB treatment prediction
ideas.description: This research fuses the investigation of missing symptoms in EHRs with ML-based prediction of inappropriate ASB treatment. It develops a machine learning framework that explicitly models the presence and impact of under-documented symptoms as a latent variable or source of bias. The model will predict treatment risk while simultaneously estimating the probability that a patient classified as 'asymptomatic' has unreported symptoms, using patterns in structured data, clinical notes, and treatment outcomes. This allows for the identification of patient subgroups where prediction models are most vulnerable to documentation gaps and enables the generation of more reliable, clinically nuanced risk assessments that account for data incompleteness.

ideas.title: Adapting Precision Agriculture's Variable-Rate Technology to Antibiotic Infusion
ideas.description: This idea explores an advanced dosing strategy for severe infections by drawing an analogy from precision agriculture's variable-rate technology (VRT). In VRT, real-time sensor data dynamically adjusts the application of inputs like fertilizer. The analogous solution is adapted by proposing a closed-loop, feedback-controlled antibiotic infusion system. This system would use continuous or frequent measurements of relevant biomarkers (e.g., drug concentrations, markers of infection severity) to dynamically titrate the antibiotic infusion rate in real time. The goal is to automatically and precisely maintain optimal pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic targets, personalizing therapy to improve efficacy and reduce toxicity, moving beyond static extended or continuous infusion protocols.

ideas.title: A Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial to Objectively Identify a Subpopulation Harmed by Diagnostic Delay
ideas.description: This research proposes a prospective, randomized controlled trial using a double-dummy, double-blind design to investigate the 'treatment paradox' in conditions like suspected UTI. Patients with negative index tests (e.g., urine cultures) but high clinical suspicion would be randomized to receive active treatment or placebo, with both groups receiving blinded dosing. The primary outcome would be a clinically assessed endpoint (e.g., symptom resolution, progression to complication) evaluated by blinded investigators. This design objectively tests whether a subset of test-negative patients is genuinely harmed by non-treatment, while the blinding eliminates the open-label bias that typically clouds such assessments, transforming an observational hypothesis into level-one evidence.

ideas.title: Apply Multi-Armed Bandit Algorithms from Machine Learning to CRE Treatment Trials
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the multi-armed bandit problem from machine learning to the challenge of evaluating multiple treatments for carbapenem-resistant Enterobacterales (CRE) infections. In the analogous field, algorithms like Thompson Sampling dynamically allocate resources (e.g., user traffic) to different options by balancing exploration and exploitation using Bayesian updating of reward probabilities. Adapted to a CRE clinical trial, a Bayesian bandit algorithm would be implemented to allocate patients among several antibiotic regimens or combination doses. As patient outcome data (e.g., treatment success, safety events) accumulates, the algorithm updates in real-time, automatically assigning a higher proportion of future participants to the arms showing superior performance, thereby maximizing patient benefit within the trial and identifying the most promising therapies with greater statistical and operational efficiency than static designs.

ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from ecology, this idea treats the scaling of ASB investigation to outpatient clinics or nursing homes as a study of clinical niches. In ecology, a species' survival strategies are compared across scaled environmental contexts like forest cores and edges. The analogous solution is to map trait adaptation and resource use in these different niches. Adapted to ASB research, this involves systematically comparing ASB treatment patterns—such as antibiotic stewardship, diagnostic pathways, and resource utilization—between the high-acuity, resource-rich 'niche' of the nursing home and the lower-acuity, rapid-turnover 'niche' of the outpatient clinic. This comparative niche analysis will identify how care delivery adapts to environmental constraints, revealing key leverage points for improving treatment appropriateness across the care continuum.

ideas.description: This study prospectively establishes a ground truth for symptom presence and severity by systematically collecting patient-reported outcomes via validated digital instruments during clinical encounters for a target condition. This primary data is then algorithmically compared to the structured and unstructured fields of the clinician-generated electronic health record (EHR) note from the same encounter. The analysis quantifies the prevalence, types (e.g., omission, under-scoring), and clinical contexts of documentation gaps, moving beyond a simple 'asymptomatic' label to a detailed discrepancy analysis.

ideas.title: Adaptive, Bayesian Closed-Loop PK/PD Modeling with Real-Time Clinical Data Assimilation for Antiviral Dosing
ideas.description: This system moves beyond static Monte Carlo simulation by creating a continuously learning, patient-specific pharmacological model. It embeds a Bayesian inference engine within clinical workflows to assimilate sparse, real-world patient data—such as opportunistic drug levels, renal function trends, and virologic or biomarker responses—directly into the PK/PD model. This adaptive framework progressively individualizes the model's parameters and uncertainty estimates, enabling dosing forecasts that evolve with the patient's changing clinical state. The output is a dynamic, evidence-informed dosing recommendation that robustly bridges the gap between population models and the unique, often unmeasured, characteristics of the individual.
