ideas.title: Negative Control Calibrated Propensity Score Analysis for Real-World Evidence
ideas.description: This approach augments standard propensity score matching on registry data with a formal negative control outcome (NCO) framework to address unmeasured confounding. Researchers pre-specify one or more negative control outcomes—events unaffected by the treatment but influenced by the same confounders—and analyze them within the matched cohort. The estimated association between the treatment and the negative control provides a quantitative bias parameter, which is then used to calibrate the primary effectiveness estimate. This creates a more robust real-world evidence system that not only balances observed covariates but also detects and corrects for residual bias, leading to more reliable causal inferences in post-approval settings.

ideas.title: Using Ecological Survey Methods to Detect Under-reported Symptoms in EHRs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the 'asymptomatic' classification in electronic health records is reliable by proposing that symptoms are often missing from structured clinical documentation, analogous to species being missed in single-method ecological surveys. Drawing from ecology, where multi-method surveys (camera traps, acoustic sensors, direct observation) are combined with statistical models (e.g., occupancy models) to correct for detection bias and estimate true species presence, this research adapts that solution. The adapted study would implement a multi-method 'survey' for symptoms in a clinical cohort by concurrently analyzing structured EHR data, unstructured clinician notes via NLP, patient-reported outcomes via digital surveys, and patient portal messages. Using statistical models adapted from ecology, the research would estimate the detection probability of each method and the true, corrected prevalence of symptoms, thereby quantifying the under-documentation in standard records and the potential misclassification of symptomatic patients as asymptomatic.

ideas.title: Leveraging Expert Intuition as a Signal for Latent Variables in Infection Diagnosis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geology and mineral exploration, where expert intuition based on subtle environmental cues has been used to find valuable deposits. The analogous solution involves formally analyzing these expert-identified patterns as indicators of latent geological structures to guide targeted investigation. Adapted to the clinical research context, we propose to treat the clinician's intuitive decision to treat for infection despite guideline recommendations as a similar expert signal. The research would systematically collect cases of such guideline-deviant decisions and use high-dimensional data analysis (e.g., machine learning on EHR data including vital signs, lab trends, nursing notes, and novel biomarkers) to uncover the latent clinical predictors—the 'geological structure'—that the intuition is responding to. This tests the core assumption that intuition is merely noise, positing instead that it is a correlate of an unmeasured, true infection subset, thereby potentially identifying new variables for future diagnostic models.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Resilience Metrics to Define Patient-Centered Clinical Cure
ideas.description: This idea applies the solution from ecosystem ecology, where endpoint measurement shifted from the removal of a stressor to the assessment of systemic resilience and functional recovery. The analogous problem of defining 'health' after a disturbance is solved by measuring multi-dimensional recovery of core services. Adapted to clinical research, this inverts the primary endpoint from microbiological or biochemical surrogates (like pathogen clearance) to a patient-centered 'clinical cure' defined by a composite index of time to restored functional capacity, physiological stability, and quality of life, thereby focusing on the patient's holistic recovery trajectory rather than a single disease marker.

ideas.title: A Multi-Center, Pragmatic Trial for Meropenem-Vaborbactam Using an Adaptive Platform Design
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from climate science's use of multi-model ensembles to solidify regional forecasts, this expanded idea proposes a novel confirmatory strategy for meropenem-vaborbactam. The analogous solution involves running multiple models to create a convergent, robust prediction. Adapted to clinical research, this translates to conducting a larger, multi-center confirmatory study not as a single monolithic trial, but as an adaptive platform trial network. This pragmatic 'ensemble' approach would enroll a broad CRE patient population across diverse sites, with pre-specified variations in sub-populations or standard-of-care comparators. The primary analysis would seek a convergent efficacy and safety signal across this network, providing a more generalizable and robust confirmation of the drug's profile for real-world use than a traditional, homogeneous Phase 3 trial design.

ideas.description: This research system overcomes the subjectivity of pure patient-reported outcomes by designing a primary endpoint that digitally verifies a return to normal function. The endpoint combines continuous, passive data from a wearable device (measuring activity, heart rate variability, and sleep) to objectively define 'return to baseline physiology' with a single, validated patient-reported question on readiness to resume normal activities. A pre-specified algorithm synthesizes these two streams into a single, reproducible binary endpoint of 'Functional Recovery,' providing the patient-centered focus required while meeting regulatory needs for objectivity, standardization, and reduced bias.

ideas.description: This research system applies causal inference techniques to observational ASB data but explicitly centers on probing and bounding the impact of unverifiable assumptions. It employs a suite of sensitivity analyses to quantify how strong an unmeasured confounder would need to be to nullify the observed effect. The approach triangulates findings across multiple causal methods (e.g., matching, IV, difference-in-differences) and incorporates qualitative or secondary data to assess the plausibility of key assumptions. The final output is a robust, transparency-focused assessment that communicates causal evidence alongside its specific vulnerabilities and confidence levels.

ideas.title: Adapting Causal Forest Algorithms from Economics for Real-World Evidence Generation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from econometrics—specifically, the 'causal forest' algorithm developed for estimating heterogeneous treatment effects from observational data—to the context of real-world evidence in healthcare. In economics, this method is used to analyze, for example, how the effect of a job training program on future earnings differs by age, education, or prior work history, using non-parametric, machine-learning-based ensemble models. Adapted to healthcare registry data, this approach would move beyond standard propensity score matching to implement an ensemble of causal trees. This would allow researchers to map the heterogeneous effectiveness of a post-approval treatment across numerous patient subgroups defined by comorbidities, demographics, or concurrent medications, providing a more nuanced, data-driven understanding of real-world comparative effectiveness without pre-specified parametric constraints.

ideas.title: Adaptive Dosing for Antivirals Using Precision Agriculture Feedback Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the closed-loop feedback control systems used in precision agriculture to the challenge of optimizing individual antiviral dosing. In precision agriculture, sensor networks monitor field conditions and crop status, feeding data to algorithms that dynamically adjust water and nutrient delivery. The analogous solution is adapted by using frequent biomarker measurements (e.g., viral load, inflammatory markers) as the 'sensor' input for a patient-specific PK/PD model. This model, coupled with Monte Carlo simulation to account for uncertainty, drives a control algorithm that recommends real-time dosing adjustments—moving beyond static, population-based regimens to a truly adaptive, patient-responsive antiviral therapy strategy.

ideas.title: Adapting Mendelian Randomization from Epidemiology for ASB Treatment Studies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from genetic epidemiology—Mendelian Randomization—to the context of observational ASB research. In epidemiology, Mendelian Randomization uses genetic variants as instrumental variables to strengthen causal inference about modifiable risk factors. The adapted approach would involve using established polygenic risk scores for traits relevant to ASB as instrumental variables within large observational datasets. This would allow researchers to estimate the causal effect of a specific environmental exposure, psychosocial intervention, or behavioral treatment on ASB outcomes, while naturally controlling for unmeasured confounding due to the random allocation of genetic variants, thereby significantly strengthening the causal claims possible from observational ASB treatment studies.

ideas.title: Triangulation of Bias: A Multi-Method Framework to Quantify and Control Investigator Assessment Bias in Pragmatic and Open-Label Trials
ideas.description: This research program accepts that perfect blinding is often unattainable and instead develops a practical framework to measure and adjust for investigator bias in open-label or partially blinded trials. It uses a multi-arm design that combines a traditional blinded arm (if possible) with open-label arms employing blinded independent central review and recorded assessment reviews. By comparing outcomes across these methodologically distinct arms, the system directly estimates the magnitude and direction of assessment bias introduced by open-label conditions. The output is a validated, quantitative model for bias adjustment that strengthens the evidence from pragmatic trials where double-dummy designs are not feasible.
